speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Marie J. Clark and J. Les Clark took place at their home in Lewiston on July 9, 1976. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: That old bachelors lived in and they mined. And I don't know what else they did living out in the woods. I remember one, there were two brothers that were twins, lived in there. And a lot of people, I mean University of Idaho has a copy of every gulp of the news that was ever printed. And then your names might be in there. So people picnicked a lot by the way of that. That was the place to go. Oh yes. And you could play on in the water if it wasn't too high. Particularly our Sunday school and church picnics were there. And I don't remember how many cabins, but there were quite a few. And there was a road that was made with the scoop shovel. You probably never saw that. They were too young. With a horse pulling and then a man guiding us behind. And that's the way they made the roads then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So this dam was taken out eventually?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I don't know whether you can see, I don't think you can see any bit of it anymore. Unless it comes, I'll go downstairs and find another picture.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now did you go into the bar up there and see that picture of the mill?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: No. I didn't see that one. You must not miss that. That is wonderful. And this is what my husband could foresee from this. What a picture it would make. And it hangs there in the bar. The long -sister lamb. I wish we had done one for us at the same time. But it's one foot by four and all in color. I wouldn't have seen that at all. Maybe you would have. But he did. It was so much attention. Of course a company would like to buy it, but it isn't for sale. This is a good picture too. That was the third largest electric mill in the world. In the paper, I think Virginia had the first, but it was the third.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: And there were a few other things in there that the dates seemed to be changed. But that was a big industry. And we used to play in the lumber files that were around them. And this little railway they had for motors, is what they called it, machines that pulled these lumber stacks. The lumber rather. It was just like a little city they could all over. And of course all the young men got those jobs. And to deliver this stack of lumber would be high to, well, I think you would call it a trailer today, I guess, behind, to the spot that they ordered to take it. And then it was stacked by the lumber pilots who made good money. And the same way to deliver it, if there was a sale of that particular lumber, because this is the way they dried it. And they would take it out and they were ready for shipping. And they had a railroad that went around the back by the planer to pick this up. And along here. In fact, I sat with the man that had the charge of that, I think, than today at the funeral.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Whose funeral was it?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Mr. Pettigrew.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember seeing the story or do you take the tribunes?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Not now. We used to, but now... The man that, you might have read it in your paper though, had the eye transplant a few weeks ago, or was in April right after I woke up. But here they are, part of them. Now this is the one, this is Nori, they were talking about. That's his wife, and this is his mother and baby. When my brother was in Japan, he called, he went to see them, and they sent those back to me, because I had Nori in school in Alcover. And here is the boy that lives in Southern California with his family. I don't know whether the name is on the back or not.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: George? George Pika?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Kaika. Kaika. It was really Kaiki.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Oh here it shows their little railroad tracks they had in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know where Charles is together too?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Yes, I knew all of them, and his daughter. Here's his snow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you remember them being like? They?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Yes, the Japanese folks. Oh very nice. But, they stayed to themselves. They would attend any civic programs, anything done by the school, they would always attend. But they did not mix with the people. I don't know whether they would. They weren't supposed to then or not, but then they were treated well and respected. That Nori was the cutest thing. Well, you know, there was so much. In a lot of places, they did come up against a lot of dislike. I mean, Oriental people were generally in the West. I mean, a lot of places they did. I don't imagine they did any off river, but I would think maybe they would be pretty reluctant to get too much involved. He worked in, well here's the mother, father, and the girl that live in Japan now. He worked in the planer, I think, and I think Mr. Segera did too. But, Kawakanese, they own the laundry, if I remember rightly. Because then somebody brought her name up, that made them Virginia too. Because Holmbergs had a store there, and they lived in an apartment behind. They had a daughter, Angie, and they couldn't keep Sarah Kawakanese out of their house. They could teach her to help her. But they were good friends, and she made friends.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: You know, there was some suspicion that Charlie Segera was working for the Japanese government while he was there. Could have been, but... You know, that they wanted to know what the bill was like, you know, later and stuff. I never heard it, and I don't know whether Les ever did or not. He came there as just a 19 -year -old, pulling work for Bill Maranow, who had the paper then. It's too bad Bill is not mentally able to give up. He was shaking my brother in the hallway of the school there by the third and fourth grade room. And he hit his head, and I'm like, hook! And I happened to come around the corner and saw him do that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was like, what would I do?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: And I was old then, I guess. Then, several years later, we, for a fire escape, they had one of these canvas slides, a brand -new one. They got to hook in the window of the second story on the right -hand side as you go into the building. And this Calkins didn't like a large family of Martins, and this one boy, I can't remember his first name. I think the last name was Martin. And he boosted him up to this slide to go down ahead of me, so I got a good look. And you know that man actually took that boy's foot and twisted it. I can remember that so well. I know Calkins was there before. Newland, Newland, King.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was he like as a teacher?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I don't know, because he handled upper grade as a teacher. You obviously didn't like him as a person. Thank God he was gone, and this Newland was very sweet to me all the time.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: There's some beautiful pictures in here. Maybe I have to go down and get that other old one. See, this one building that's standing is what Dad built when he was there, when I came down here to school, and this mill was being built. This is my brother Fred and me and my little sister. She only lives seven years in Little Brother Land. My folks had the home on the corner. As you go into Elk River, as you enter by the school crossing, if you just look right straight ahead, where that greenhouse, I think you've got it being that. My folks owned that place, but that front porch was not enclosed then. Let's see, it's one reason I had this up here too. I wanted to show you what it looked like. My folks, this is the Sunken Garden, trees and lawn. Oh, that's really pretty. It was a show place, and my mother did all that work. But it's a mess now. She took pictures during the summer and all the time. But that gives you an idea how people at that time, together, you met Arva Ann Monroe up there, and she has a beautiful yard.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened to your little sister that she died?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Meningitis. At that time, Meningitis, three days, she was gone. She had the non -contagious kind, which is fatal. If you live through it, it is contagious. I didn't know that until we owned a newspaper down in California. We became acquainted with some parents and Mary's husband had Meningitis as a man after they were married. I was really interested because it was a rare thing to live through that. That's when she told me, and of course since then I've learned it and I've heard it many times. We don't know the doctor's course. That's back in 1923. They had a serum in Chicago, but it would take two days to fly it out here. Well, of course, she wouldn't have lived that long. Then, too, if she had, she probably wouldn't have been right because it was so much in the brain. That would have been hard. She was such a brilliant little girl. At breakfast, it was in January, and therefore she couldn't start to school with her friends in September. It would be September of 1915. She was born in 1916. But when she did, the next year, she had two grades in a room. She made both grades. She learned a lot, though, at home from us. She was a real sharp little thing. It was to really get a blow to the family. Oh, to the whole town, to have a little child go over something like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She says, Vivid, of course, today, isn't it?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: As it was that day, those three days. She had a bad accident with another little girl. They used to coast, in order, from the hill where the Catholic Church was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did anybody point that out to you where it had been standing, up on the hill?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I knew that it was there in town, but I didn't know exactly where. Well, if you were facing the schoolhouse, it would be that street where Mrs. Fine is. It went all the way up the top. That's where the church was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: We had wide steps, but, say, shall we?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Not very high. You could coast down there. You could start up pretty high. But most of the time, these younger children, well, even I, you would start at the bottom step, which is right where the one house, well, there are two houses up there, I guess now, the one house. You could start that and go a long ways. You could start there, rather. She had been forbidden to go coasting there, but she couldn't stand in the course with all her little friends there. So she went. And she just pleaded with Prudence to let her take her turn with Tooki. So Tooki and Prudence did. Very, very, very close little friends. It was just amazing how those little kids were at that time. She was eight years younger than I. And Darn it, the little spits dog didn't run in front of them when they got to the crossing. And to avoid hitting yet, they were going belly buster, if you know how that is in coasting.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you? Just laying flat out?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well, the gider, the one who steers, lays flat. And we in Elk River kneeled between their legs and over the body. We called it belly buster, but a lot of places, they played flat on top of the other. But we didn't do that. And Tooki hit the telephone pole, and it threw both the girls, of course, into the pole. And Tooki bled internally. She was pretty sick. And my sister, I believe she probably bled, too. And they should have rushed her right off to Spokane, both of the girls.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But why?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: They didn't, I don't know. And Helen seemed to get better, but Mrs. Shimla lived right there, and they had closed friend of my folks, so she just picked her up and carried her off in the woods. Well, she recovered from that. That was before she went to school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that right?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Yeah. And then she fell on the ice, backwards one time, and hurt her head, and complained a lot. And the third one was just before school was out. The desk in school, and cut her head over here. But they say that all those things could have contributed to it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But how do we know?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Science has come a long way since then. We had a very good doctor that recognized the disease right away. But it was a horrible thing. Only did was just to wait for her to die. Pretty tiny thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you know at times that she was rational because she asked for me, where is Marie?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: And they came out and got me, because I was in and out anyway all the time. And then one time I was in the kitchen, and here she was, walking out of the bedroom on her tiptoes and nightie. She was thirsty. She knew where to go get that drink. So it was one of those things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You say it was quick, or it didn't take long?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Three days. And it's a violent headache. It's a terrible headache. They just spent all of their time standing over and watching and doing what they could. You say that she and her friends were really close. That was unusual for kids to be as close as they were. Well, I don't think so. Because it goes with the neighborhood, I feel. Now these little girls play together right there in our neighborhood. However, the two -keeper driver was the daughter of the postmistress. As they called her in, she lived towards downtown. They lived behind the post office in an apartment. They were beside one or the other. That building was converted to many different things inside through the years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you figured that the Elk River was the kind of place that our kids really did know each other especially well?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Yes, and we commented on that when we were all together. Oh, yes. I just got so thrilled. I do. Any of these Elk River people I see. Now that the golden anniversary for the village, that was just hugging and kissing all the time. All these people hadn't seen each other for so long. And there's a couple of Italian women living here. And one I haven't seen since I came back. It's just the way my life has been here. Very interesting. I like it. But when she recognized who I was, she just kissed me all over my face and pulled me down and kisses me some more. I just have to stop with them and see their little thing. Well, tell me a little bit of what that meant to be so close. How the kids were so close in Elk River. I think more like family. A great deal of compassion for each other. That's going to be good for kids, I guess. But we had our spats and fights. Sure, maybe mad at somebody for a little while and get over it. But now you take those three men in my class that were here. And then there was one more came from Denver who had graduated ahead of us. And they were just so close. They had done so many happy things together as children up there and boys growing up all over the woods. Keith was, well, all four of them just to watch him. So happy to be together. And one of them is almost blind. So when this I wrote an asking about, well, what I said was, I'm wondering if you have given any thought to what an important year this is. And they had all three men. The other ladies in classroom. And they answered right away and they answered also by telephone. Well, this one that is going blind from Herpy, you know, from.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: To me, like, when I was struck with when I was at Elk River that that for that little program they had just there, it seemed to me that Elk River was a very close town. I don't know why it should be that it should seem closer to the other town. Everybody there. Well, maybe I shouldn't say everybody because they're old people came in. We're not so happy. They sense the same thing. And while they live there, they like it. And every child loved it. There were a lot of things to do in any ways to make your own fun. We went swimming in the river at that time, bitter cold. And we could ice skate everywhere. I shouldn't say every winter if it was real cold and then it turned warm enough to snow, it might snow a foot or two and you would be able to skate. But some of the big boys in the Lord was a big boy when I was a little girl. They would go down there and there were several Scandinavians in town, lots of them. So they were skaters and clamped skates on. The Scandinavian had skates that were made of wood and that part fit on the sole of your shoe and then they had their big curved blades. They made them themselves. And they would shovel paths through the snow on the ice so we could skate around in all these places. My mother always went with me. She'd take Chong and a friend of mine. And I would go along. There were many other girls that skated like that. But my mother was such a good sport and so broad -minded and she'd take us so we could do everything. Then when my husband came in 1923 and they had ice, he never lets my brother lose his down. His skates, he didn't know it. He had sent for his skates in Canada. And they were lying in the depot but Mr. Copeland hadn't notified and they were there. So he didn't have his own skates and he asked my brother if he could borrow his and he said, no, he afraid he would ruin them. Of course that's before we knew this really. But I never felt alone with his skates to us. And that whole weekend we had practically the whole town down there watching him skate. Beautiful skater. Hockey player. I think he's the best ice skater that ever came to town. He'd come from Canada. Yes, he born in Manitoba and raised mostly in a trail. He played on a trail, hockey teams, in the proudness championship one year. I don't know how he did so much. Well, for one thing, he was real sharp in school. He started at four years of age. They had room and they let him go to school, in country school. So he was through high school and through his trade of six years before he ever came to this country. But I was telling him he could have done a whole lot more. He was so self satisfied with what he did when he kept up. He went to school for a lot of things in Oakland and San Francisco. And when he couldn't work on his feet anymore because of the lighting trees, he had to learn the minotype. He did. He's a terrific operator even yet. He's an honor every day. So we've had a better life, I guess, than a lot of average people as far as things that have been interesting in our life. Because that's getting away from Elk River again. But our school life up there, I appreciate the fact that we were required to take university interests of education. That isn't right. Preparation. So we were qualified to enter university. Anybody who goes to school from Elk River, if he's a student at all, can pass all those examinations. That you have to take. Well, I don't know if that way today or not. I think that I heard something recently that Elk River students... Created high. Created very high. Just recently on the state -wide basis. Well, you know the mayor of Lewiston is an Elk River -born boy that we've already begun. And like we mentioned, there's many highly successful professionals. Got their start in Elk River.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he Harry Adams' son?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: His son. He was at the Golden Anniversary.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was Harry as much of a character as George says he was?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well, you know, I never saw Harry in a lot of those comics that you hear about. Because I guess I was too little and he worked with George Smalls. And I'm sure he was. I know he played a lot of jokes on people all the time. And he was a Lunder Cruiser too, if I remember right. Oh, that offs... Oh, wait a minute. Maybe I'm getting that part mixed up with Eddie Erickson. Eddie was a Cruiser. Eddie Erickson. Well, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did Harry Adams work, did they say, on the poem? The pool?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: But I think he worked some at the mill. I remember. He never owned it, but he worked in it. And we had four at one time there. Four pool halls. Even five. That main... Front Street, from Main Street to the Red Building at the other end of the block, had the pool hall, the Wilson Hotel, which housed the barbershop, one barbershop. And then there was a big building beside it that was a pool hall. And there was a bunk house, which in the beginning was the church and the school. No, I didn't attend school. By the time I was old enough to go, they had built the one that stands today. And then the office building. Another thing they had next to the print shop was a lumber business. A lumber... I don't think they called it a yard. It was all enclosed. But they sold and shipped lumber out of Elk River. Whether they were connected exactly with the potlatch or not, I don't know. And I don't know who would know. Well, George Smalls would know.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: And... When you're talking about the pool halls, it makes me wonder how your parents felt about kids being around the lumberjacked downtown, that sort of thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do they have any...?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: No fear. There was nothing that ever happened with a lumberjack molesting a child in that town. Never did I know of. Of course I was on. I was 35 years old. But I don't think so. Children could run any place. And if you were out on the road, especially between Elk River and Boeville in Moscow, in the beginning when they had a road to get... I wouldn't say a road if there wasn't a highway, getting trouble. They'd just come right down from the woods and help you.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: But today, well, not as Elk River is today, we wouldn't be afraid. I remember one summer, we'd teenage girls slept outside on the lawns every night, mostly at our house. Pull old mattresses around and sleep out there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the drinking? Were they very good?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Are parents very concerned about kids seeing... We never did. Kids wouldn't be drinking, but I mean... Well, they probably tasted it in their teens, in the moonshine and stuff, but I was never in that kind of company. And I don't know if any of the girls slept there that did. Not at any time I was... Well, I was actually thinking more about the lumberjacks in town, you know, about all kind of seeing that kind of stuff. Because I've known that sometimes parents seem to be concerned about that sort of thing, you know. I think it was different, quite a bit different. Kids accepted the pool hall, and they could go in and get a candy bar. And each one of those men who owned the pool hall was very good to each other. We had to deliver meat, as you heard me say, all our school life. And we were not allowed to accept money or gifts, maybe a cookie or donut, something like that, for delivering. But we could have a candy bar from Joe Pavish or Ed Allen. That's how strict my dad was. And he had far more to drink than he should.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is this going on here?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I won't say that.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: But then when they had prohibition, like... I get mixed up on this, prohibition between the donuts, but it's not legalized. Well, of course, the thing at home, moonshine and everything. But it still wasn't rowdy or anything to be concerned about. And there were times when some person who was wanted would come to town, and our sheriffs and police were very good at spotting. But lots could have gone on that I didn't know, too. Now, another thing they said in one article that Mr. Leaper was a drug star. He was not. He had nothing to do at all in any way with the drug star. I don't know what you saw that story that came out in the Clearwater Tribune. Ma of mine showed me a copy of it, my glance at it. I haven't actually read the article yet. And I sent it on to my brother.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: He's the only member of his family in the United States. And he learned his trade in Germany. Well, I don't crazy do. It's four or five, six years. And he barn stormed around Europe for experience, which they were required to do. He says he made sausage for the Kaiser, but I don't know if he did or not. And he decided, I guess, that he wanted to come to the United States, and he had an uncle in Kansas, so he did. He went there first. I don't know how long he stayed. I don't think he even stayed a year. And then he went on to Denver, and Denver had quite a settlement of German people. He'd been, uh, put on to return for mine, and, uh, all the rare bars, and stuff, too, and made sausage there in Denver. But his health failed when he married my mother. Her cousin introduced him to me. He was a butcher, too. And for his health, he had to get out of Denver. He landed in Palouse with Henry Burns as his boss. And I was seven months old, and I stayed there until I was four. I was just four when they left here. And Fred was born there. And he went into business for himself, and there was another meat market in town at that time, too. But it didn't last very long. I don't know whether Dad brought him out or not. The one building that stands along there, that pink -colored building, two -story building, well, that was where Dad had his first market. Then right beside it, with no more than a foot between the two, was this second building that he rented first, and we lived above it. And then he eventually bought that, and he had bought this lot on the corner where he built.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know what made him decide to go to Elk River?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well, yes. Uh, the fact that he would be his own boss, an independent, and it was a new town getting started. But I think those were the reasons. So then he stayed there until 1960. 1912 to 1960. And he, well, those last years he was in Elk River, he didn't own it. He had to sell because he incited in both arms and shoulders. And he couldn't work, and so when he was sick we had to dispose of the new market.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was your mother German as well?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Yes, but born in Denver, and her parents came over here from Brainerd. But they met here. They were not married when they came. My grandmother had a twin sister who came with her from Germany. So their name was Peterson. Grandpa changed his name, which would have been Hoting. His brother used the name of Hoting, but for some reason he changed his. And he sailed around the world three times. He was a sailor eight years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He could even, he owned the property that this Denver capital stands on today, or the call -out, did it call around the capital?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: It stands on there one time. That's how they happened to come to this part of the country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When he started in Elk River, did he start with a pretty good -sized market, or did he take him a long time to build up?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: He started very well. It was hard work. Mom had been helping. They would make the sausage and hamburgers and stamp them until two or three o 'clock in the morning, grinding it all by hand. And he sold to the camps at that time. Then, I don't know, Benny was able to buy an electric grinder when that came in. But Benny got that. Of course, it helped a lot. And then we had to help too. He made these winnies, sausages. And Fred and I, we had to take turns turning that handle. And then a dad would twist the winnies and the sausages. Just perfect. Which one every time, you know, perfect size, length. It was fascinating. Interesting. He had a big back room. Chickens. He raised chickens and imported them live too. Then we would have to help clean those chickens. Just little kids. And had benches to work on. I couldn't eat chicken for years and years and years. I smelled wet feathers all the time. And I could just see if he would scall these chickens and lay them on the bench. And then he'd show us how. He has a big hand. He'd rip half the feathers off at one time. And here we were, these little kids, picking that stuff and mom too. Oh boy. See, kids were expected to work, expecting to help. In my family, not all families, because we didn't like it. When we had to deliver meat where there were kids that could go and get it. Many a time we broke trails to school, through the snow, delivering that meat. My mother was very much opposed to it. And the dad didn't lose his head very much. It was when we looked back and she too, she and I talked about a lot. We should not have worked so hard. We had lots of playtime too, as far as that goes. And if we were out playing, dad wouldn't interrupt us to deliver the meat less of a fairy and clock. They'd say, well no, this is his playtime. We don't have to wait a while.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: But I forgot what I was looking up for.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that was basically old country?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: More so, yes. I think so. Oh yes. I think he'd do it, I do. But it's the way he'd be. Just like others, he's giving you a show. Kids, that's what you have kids for. But he should have paid us so much a week. And each year increase it to teach us how to handle money. When I got old enough to learn that they got allowances, I didn't know what they meant at first. And that's what he should have had. And mom agreed too. And then Fred and I grew tall, so young. That's one reason I claim that I have round shoulders, is because I was tired of working hard like that. And I would slump and they'd try to make me straighten up and I wouldn't bother. Besides delivering, you really were expected to help the grind, all that stuff. Well, when we were very small, yes. But after dad got the electric grinder, no, we didn't.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: He could handle it all himself. Of course the boys, both brothers had to learn that. Fred lost these two fingers on the 4th of July.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When he was still a kid?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: My mother's brother was there visiting when that happened.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was he doing?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Pushing the meat into the big grinder. It was a big thing. And he didn't get his fingers out and he wasn't supposed to do that. He didn't use the paddle. The paddle setter would be used. And of course on the 4th of July, when he was doing... The thing is... The richer dad had it. And that Christmas, this is all sawdust. We slipped into the market, but it wasn't this one. It was when we first moved there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was he?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Two and a half, I guess. And I was about four. Or I was four, past four. And we scooped up the sawdust and threw it all at me. And we threw it... He had pickle barrels and sauerkraut here. And there were big barrels. And we threw it in there. And my mother said, it's a good thing we had company, because when your dad came in and told her what had happened, if that lady hadn't been visiting, you would have very sure gotten a foot thing. But by the time she left and he cooled off, he didn't touch us. But he told us we wouldn't get anything for Christmas. And we did. And Christmas Eve, we went to the Baldersons and Mary was an only child. And she got a little piano and she got a table and chairs. And Santa Claus came in and...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where do you think Fred and I were?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Under the table. We did. She had the tablecloth that hung clear to the floor. And there we were. I was there. And Santa Claus came out. Who was her father. And knowing this story, he knew we were there. He found us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are you the little boy and girl that threw the sawdust in the pickles and the need?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: He is. You'd be better than to cry. We didn't get a thing that night. But the next morning, we had some toys on our bed. Oh, he really taught us some hard lessons. We don't regret it. We figured we were better people for it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it really that bad what you'd done throwing sawdust in?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: No, it was just... Well, it was bad enough, yes, because that was darn hard to get out of the pickles and the sau... sauerkraut. And he got it off of the beef easily, but it just had a terrible time getting it off the reveal.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well, did you done it just...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: just the heck of it?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: At that age, how do you know? How would I remember? What you were doing it for?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I'm sure we knew better, especially I. It was fun, I guess. He wasn't around. You know, I have heard of... of...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know Frank Rocky?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Very loud. He told me how his father used to come in and play Santa Claus. It was more real, like it should be, than it is today. Lesson I fill each other a stocking every year at Christmas time, or a little boot. And that's more fun than anything as a part of our Christmas. Chewing gum and tarlet articles we need and always some real nice thing. This was in the bottom of my mind two weeks, two years ago. Diamond in the rough. And we enjoy it. If we were going to have company for Christmas, like his cousins who were here the other day, and they came to spend Christmas with it, they warned them to bring something for each person to go in the stocking and to boot, whatever I fix. I always thought that was a lot of fun.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was Christmas like in Elk River?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well, it wasn't as it is today by any means. We always had a Christmas tree and candles. And no electric lights, of course. And I think everybody had the same as we. We'd hover around the stove for those of us who had no fireplace. There weren't too many fireplaces in town. And, oh, of course the big thing of all was that Christmas program at the church every Christmas. And you go home. We always had our Christmas in the morning, not at night. And I think it was just okay. A lot of fun. We didn't know what a dime store was. They picked up all these items that you can today, and the drugstore handled everything. Mr. Morris got very nice things in for Christmas. Good dishes for children and good toys of all kinds. In fact, one Christmas friend and I woke up and on the bed was a box like this. I don't remember whether Helen was a baby then or not. We had her yet, but we shared the same room. And we got a set of animals, I can show you. I still have some of them, that were jointed. It was a circus set, and each animal could do stunts. There were two chairs, two ladders, a barrel, and then that big tub thing, all of wood. Now these things are made yet today in Germany and very costly. The last time I saw a set was at I Magnins in Oakland. And that was several years ago, that was $75. And I think what I saw in Germany was $175 today. And there were two accused clones. I had one left. I took his little suit off and cleaned and washed it and put it back on him. And that I want to stay in my family, but we only have one little grand niece. John had three children. And I want to be sure that they would like to preserve them before I hand them down. I have a tiger and an elephant downstairs and a mule.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You got all that at Christmas, that one Christmas?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Yes, all these things came in this one box, it would be about the size.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For the two of you?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Yes. Oh, I think I got a doll besides. One year I got three dolls. Nice big ones. And I don't know why, I never preserved them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened to those dolls?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: You know, you're talking about your father and your mother. It sounds as though, this is something that we've really been interested in, you know, finding out how kids were raised in those days. Parents' attitudes. It sounds like your mother was a lot more lenient towards you than your father. Yes. However, he never objected to anything she did for us. What she bought, how she clothed us or anything. And we were nicely dressed. They had very hard times over the First World War. They'd lost everything. And it was real hard for them. But Christmas and birthdays were the only time you received anything special. Then the Fourth of July was the other big day, an Easter. When we had Easter, Mom would color at least two dozen eggs, and Dad hid them. If Easter were early, they'd be hidden around in the meat market or the back room. If it was late and we didn't have any snow, we'd have it outside. Then after we found all the eggs, we would re -hide them ourselves. All day long, when we went to Sunday school, we never missed. And we always had new Easter clothes. We hid and hide and seek the eggs, let's say, and took turns.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were a lot of the families in town doing the same thing?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I don't know. I guess they did. I really don't know. This was family. This was us, our family. We did it that way. I really don't know. She'd ask Joy if they did it that way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How are decisions made in a family between your parents? Did you probably give your mother much say -so about things?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: The German had stubborn... That's the whole country again. That's true of Scandinavian countries. That's a whole country. Give her your Italians.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: But she was a far better businesswoman. Her dad was a businessman and a far better, oh, a good manager. Excellent executive. She knew she took ahold of anything like ladies' aid and said she was the president seven years. I'm very successful with it. But she said she got good cooperation. Everybody liked to work for it, and they said they did. She was willing to take the responsibility.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she help manage the business?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: But she worked in it. Dad said she was the best looking butcher in town. Of course, she told me one. But he never let me behind the counter. Never could I cut anything. The only time. Because he knew I was a little scatterbrained and I'd lose a finger. But he knew when I'd been in there because I loved liver sausage. And I would slice it straight down instead of a slant. But he never got after me before it.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: But what we had was good. You know, it was good things. It was good toys. It was Christmas and birthdays. And they were important. Just as important to Dad as they were to us. Do you think that, you know, I guess I have to wonder whether your mother would feel,

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: she could have done a better job at some of that stuff than he could. Like managing, that sort of thing. She just had to keep her mouth shut about it.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well, I guess she knew when I was. I was afraid I'd miss something over here or over there. I didn't want to miss anything. So I didn't concentrate hard enough. And we never had to do a lot of memorizing in school. Like my husband, oh my goodness in Canada, it's terrific what they taught in memorization. It was easy for Fred. John was a good student.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long would it have been?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Real good, I think. Another thing we were raised, as I say,

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: well, strict, very firm by Dad. And because he was in business, we had to watch what we did. We weren't free to do a lot of things other children did. Somebody would talk about it. You're not supposed to do this or that. So there was a damper. I wanted to do business to make a difference about this. Dad's idea.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: And another thing, you know, people will run and paddle everything they possibly can. And he's mom used to get mad at me because he would listen to somebody else first. And if you get a spanking in school, you get one at home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Like what kind of things, for instance, that were other kids able to do that you?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: It seemed to have a lot more freedom to go do things than we did. We could take part in anything that was supervised and anything in school and sent to school and everything. We could fly a lot. But I don't know. I don't know how to explain it. At home, too. Oh, one thing, we had to be pretty quiet when we lived above the meat market. Too much noise downstairs at certain times we could fly. I know Alan had a nice big buggy for her. And Hall ran the full length of the building to the living room. And all three of us could get in that big buggy and ride back and forth. We couldn't do that from four to six. It wouldn't be the hour.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would the kind of play be that you would do as you were growing up, say as a teenager with other girls and other people?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well, we did a lot of hiking, I'd say, and picnicking and mushrooming in the spring. Puggle -bearing. Of course we went swimming in that cold river. A lot of nature. No, that is something we didn't have, was the nature study when it was right there in our back door. Since I lived up there, or especially after living in Nevada City, California, where we did, and I saw what these artists did with what I had in my yard, I wonder why in the world we didn't think of that. Years ago, all of the reeds we could have made and all these fancy little things that you'd pay quite a bit in the store for today, they were just rocks to paint them and all that sort of thing. Or make a little plaque and have part of the bark standing up. Find some other little pine cones or any little barrier that would harden you could use.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you just enjoyed it, but didn't look at it?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: We never thought of it. We used to make jewelry out of flowers, silver blossoms, rings and necklaces. We'd braid them. And crowns for our heads. Necklaces. But of course it didn't last very long. Those are ways we kept busy. Oh, I always had a playhouse, a real nice playhouse. Another way to fix it up for me. Another girl's did too. We even made playhouses out of great big cartons.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Played school.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Mine, well, behind the meat market was a building that was built halfway up with lumber, I guess, paper, and then the rest was tenting. Canvas. And the roof was tenting with one room. She fixed that up when nobody was living there. And we played in that, with girlfriends and all. And some of the other girls had nice little playhouses their fathers could build for them that my dad couldn't do anything with a hammer and a nail. I never had time anyway. And I remember one of the girls had a, she had five brothers, but at that time she only had two herby and chimp. And Herb would play with us and we'd get these big cartons and put them on their backyard and cut doors in them. We'd have to crawl in and out. But, well, we just didn't have cars. We had wagons, no bicycles. We couldn't ride a bicycle up there easily in those days, in the board sidewalks. When Fred got his, I was in the sixth grade, and he saved for, I don't know how long, months and months, maybe longer, to buy a Ranger bicycle with $75 then. Of course I had to drive it. I'd do everything he did for it. I played ball and I skated and we couldn't have roller skates either. No place to roller skate. So I learned to ride the bike. I took some horrible spills. And then I rode my boyfriends on it too. And John told Dad about it and I didn't get the bicycle anymore, which was ripe.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I had no business riding these kids on the bike, wasn't it?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: An expensive bike like that. I don't resent it one bit. It was ripe. And after falling and skinning myself all over, I didn't care. The fella that I used to coast with, I was in sixth grade and he was in eighth. I remember riding him. He probably could have ridden himself, but of course I wasn't supposed to let anybody just ride it.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Winter time was a lot of fun. In the snow and building forts and coasting. One winter we had, it was when there was snow, woods, back of the schoolhouse or up towards the cemetery, it had all been burned out. All of that had all been burned over. Well, completely around Elk River it had been burned over, but not all at once. And there were lots of stumps. Apparently, the woodsmen went up and cut them off. But we had very deep snow. It rained a little bit and it froze. We could just start way up there and we could go up and down all these bumps and hills and slide coast for a long time. As long as the cold lasted. Must have been great. We had lots of fun. And the big fellas, and here's the Scandinavians again, they would build their own bobswets. The Hendrickson boys had some pretty nice ones. They had two. And they were generous. They were good. Two of the little kids. I know. They coasted down the side of the hill where the Morrises lived. And they'd start up at the top there and go all the way down to the railroad track. And that was one of the favorite hills. We lived over there in this neat market, about this neat market. So I would go and they'd let me ride and they'd pull me back up the hill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that how you would mostly be on bobsleds if the kids would ride?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Or be on single slides?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Everyone had, well I shouldn't say everyone, but we did. We had our own.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And the competition, who had the fastest flexible flyer?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Between the boys, chiefly. I can't imagine what happened to Les or what he didn't call. But I think the winter was just great fun too. And the summers were all right. And the people would get the picnics and, well, through the church they would get the youngsters together and take them on these picnics. And down the dam, that was their favorite place then. And when I was older in high school we would go up towards the camps. And see where they call that place up there. Walked five miles to go on a picnic. And just young people. Those that were here and others that were in school. Some of those fellows are gone. They're dead already.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How big would a group would go?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Eight, ten, well maybe fifteen, twenty. And we had lots of house parties in the wintertime. We'd go find out which mother would let us have a party. And we'd play spin the bottle and post office and all those kind of games. Mom was real generous. She let us have parties a lot. And she would have very nice birthday parties for us. Always. Another friend and I had a birthday four days apart. So we got older in high school. We'd celebrate them together. And that was nice too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you play, did you do those games like? Run, sheep, run all over the hills this summer?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I was thinking of the play party games like how we would skip to my loo and all those kind of games. We did that quite a bit right at Pullaway. Every recess which was once in the morning and once in the afternoon.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Pullaway?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well you had a base there and a base here. And you had to, whoever was it, had to try to catch the person that was crossing from one side to the other. Well if he was caught he became one of the it's too. And there was one man, a fellow called Eddie Hendrickson, who was the fastest runner at that time. Nobody could ever catch him. One time he was out there being real smart. And he didn't know that I was also it because I stood too close to the base. And I just quietly got up behind him out in the middle of the playground and tapped him quickly three times. And I stood there, he was so surprised. Well I was a hero and a heroine then for a long time as I caught Eddie Hendrickson. And things like that we did. It sounded like a pretty good spread of ages would be playing together too. Oh the full eight grades did in that playground.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Outside of school would the kids mix over a pretty good age?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I think, well yes I think they did, but they stayed more in their own neighborhood. They didn't run all around all together all the time. I know when we lived over at the meat market while I played with the kids on the next block and we were talking about this at the reunion. And the church had a high sidewalk. So we could play under it. We played under there so much, especially summer too because we were not in the direct sun. And we talked about that and that was a lot of fun. I don't know what we did. We built things and boy we played house. The boys would be in the coffin and brought some mama. And one time they were talking and I asked Ben and Keith if they remembered this. We called them fellows and a boyfriend and a fellow. I said, well everybody has a fellow but me. They just hollowed but they didn't remember it. I thought it was just real smart. I have a boyfriend too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that important to girls?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I think so. But neither one remembered. I can remember Keith. You do too Keith.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were dating, what did you do? If you were going out together, what would be the sorts of things you would do?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Oh, walking, hiking mainly in the beginning of high school because none of those boys had a car. And the first place you walked in the spring would be the railroad track and the snow was gone from that first. When we learned to dance, we got to dances when we were old enough. I wasn't allowed to go until I was 15 and then my dad didn't approve of it. But he let me go and only he'd say, you'll be home at 10 o 'clock or I'll come and get you. You can remember that finger and that get you. I was home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where were the dances?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: At the old gym. Well that was, the gym of course was not there when the town was built. The first dance halls were above the store that was parallel, ran parallel across the street to the dad's meat market. The building was dance now. And then the other dance hall was the Oddfellas Hall which doesn't stand at all. It isn't there at all anymore. It was below the drugstore by the alley. I don't think there would be no place else they'd ever dance that I know of.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would it be a local group, an Elk River group that would play?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Yes, oh yes. Gee, we had a wonderful town band. I saw a picture of it. Yeah, I hear it is.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They played the dances?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Uh huh. Well no, not the band entire. But some of, oh this is what I wanted you to see. Some of these fellows played in the orchestra. It would be maybe five and six pieces. Women played the piano and they had some good piano players then. And Bill Marano played a clarinet and this trombone player played. Kennedy played in that. And this was our band master, Mr. Sibbets. And there was a band in all the little towns and they would travel on the train and have competitions and things. Or go for 4th of July but they would all come home so darn on. Plastered. The wives would get mad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of music?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Oh, that's good old time music that we still like. Waltzes and two steps and froxtrots and three steps. They played those at the dance. All the popular tunes of that time. Well let's see, some of them, when I got married, let's see I can think that far, we've got loads of music in here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you get married?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: 1930. But I was trying to think, at that time popular tunes were tiptoe through the tubes in that era. But before that there were many and many other popular tunes. But this was really popular music. It wasn't that it wasn't like country music. No, it wasn't this fiddling that you hear today in country here. My husband plays that too. He was out last night. And no, we didn't have it. But all around this area here, and up in these farms in the prairies, they never let it die. Les had it in Canada and he played all those Scotch tunes and he earned more money playing one night for the dances and trail than he did all week on his trade, learning his trade. 15, 16 dollars a night and he would make, hardly make that a week, a dollar a day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was playing tunes, what was he playing?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: He played all these popular tunes and he played Scotch tunes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did he play?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Quadrules and whatever. Oh, he plays the violin. He has three and he has a big bass file downstairs.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: And, well, in Elk River they didn't use those but what they had were a lot of Scandinavian dances and the Hombos and the Walkses. One year when I was going to school here, I had a dance in Elk River. And I wasn't going to have to be home that weekend but they decided they'd have a real good time. Some of the young fellows organized it. Well, Les took the place of the man that was playing the violin and people remember this very well, a lot of them. And he played a tune, a Scandinavian tune for the Swedes and the Norwegians and they clapped and screamed for more and more. It was just wonderful. And the man that was the depot agent at that time didn't like Les. Though he knew Les and I were going together and they were at our house every Sunday with two mean kids. He said, oh, look at that guy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Does he think he can play?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: And of course, Copeland couldn't dance with beams anyway. And when he saw what happened, he saw Les go up there with the violin and he went in when he played and how it brought down the house and how happy it made everybody. He was at the shop the next morning to apologize to Les when he arrived to work. He told him what he did. He says, I shot off my face but I had to hand it to you. That was wonderful. He has a lot of good music in him. Now this is what I wanted you to see, the old clock house. And nobody could go through that yard going through the clock house first and punching. And that was during the war, first World War, when we had Wobblies up there. And here's the mill way back here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know why he didn't like Les?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well, oh sure. One thing, Les was too mature for his age. He was too sharp. They thought he was showing off. It really wasn't. It was just his natural way. It's the way he learned in Canada. And of course if he said, well we did it this way or that way, then people would resent it. But he has all the good qualities he wanted.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I know that the German people had a rough time during the war. You bet. Now Dad and Mr. Miller were the only German speaking people in town. And the second war was bad enough too. And they had to stop speaking German at home. And we were learning to speak, especially the little verses Dad taught us. And you know, before he passed away here, he said, oh yes, you used to say this. And he rattled off all these little jingles and things that he taught us. But we missed the language.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that thing off? It isn't off?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: No. But this is part of the history.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened to people in that time?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well, for one thing, meat had to be rationed. And the preserving processes were not what they are today. So my dad lost thousands of dollars in just meat that he couldn't keep. He had an ice house every winter, which they did every winter, but you couldn't always skate on the snow on top. Well, they'd have to shovel snow off. And these men would cut the ice enough to fill his ice house. And that's how he preserved the meat. And this ice house they have right in the shop, the ice box that we made in the shop, well, above it is where he stored all this ice. You know, it was a lot of work. Pull that out of the big ice house and bring it in and then lift that up there above their heads, big chunks of ice. But he couldn't sell the meat that he had because of the ration.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that it?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Yes. He could only sell it so many days a week. I think it was two days or three days. No more than three days a week could just sell meat. And that meant a lot of the meat went bad. Oh, sure. Well, you know, you had to ship it in by train, too. It came wrapped in paper and gunny sacks. You're bringing back a lot of things that I remember.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So how come that, I mean, they wouldn't let him just sell it to keep it from going bad?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Oh, no, no, that was the law. They had to be very careful what they said and what they did being Germans. Well, that's the thing. I can't understand that. I mean, just because you're a German. Well, no, not because you're German, exactly. Now, the people were not cruel to us. Only my school teacher was me. But no, if they were mean to my mother and father in words, I didn't hear it. And no doubt there were a lot of people that made lots of remarks. But, you know, there were lots of Serbians and Austrians and they all speak the German language, too. And they were in nearly all backshores. They got lots of meat, but I think you could only buy it on certain days. And very few of them married. They had Serbians, Austrians, Greeks, Italians, Japs, Swedes, Norwegians, and a turnover. But you say they would, I mean, I had heard this, that people couldn't speak German on phone lines, you know. They made the farming farmers. Frank Brockie was talking to me about that, you know. They didn't like to hear his mother talk on the phone to, you know, urge her. That could be true, but we didn't have that many in Elk River or enough phones, either, at that time. He did, too. Well, I'm sure. Yes, he had no one to speak to, but Mr. Miller, who was an old gent that over there, my father, and he was a section boss. And I can remember him. He moved down to Julietta and he raised watermelon and candelopin in the summertime after my dad could get a car, which was the year that Helen passed away, I guess, or the year before, on old Mitchell. We'd drive down there and fill up the car, or somebody might bring it to him that was coming up from Julietta, and he would sell these for Mr. Miller. Well, they could speak German. But at the moment, I can't think anyone else in town who spoke German. There were people with a German background and German blood in them, but not from Germany like dad was. He should have settled in Wisconsin or even up in North Dakota, where they have horrible weather. Well, look at Lawrence Well. He never forgot his German. In those German settlements, I doubt if anyone stopped speaking that language. And at least in their homes, they still had it because it's difficult for the older ones to suppose, the grandparents and such.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think your father felt that he had to prove his patriotism, like the Liberty Bonds?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I'd understood that some people were really pressured, you know, felt they had to buy it. He had some, but not many, because he didn't have the money to buy them. If he was pressured to do it, I don't know. That was only in the third and fourth grade then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your teacher was mean to you? About being German during that time?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Don't call me one. Another little thing, you should say. She's still alive. She lived in Spokane. I have three teachers living here in this area.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who taught you?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: As old as I am. There are teachers here. Mrs. Lillard, they had her golden anniversary. And Mrs. Baysinger over in Clarkson. And she was my home act. And then the music teacher was not connected with the school. She was down here three blocks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did this teacher do it, you know, criticizing you and talking to the other kids?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Oh, right. Well, of course. And I never liked her. And I always said, well, if I ever become a schoolteacher or whatever I do, I'm going to look nice. If people have to look at me, I'm going to look nice. Because she was so sloppy. And her posture was terrible. She gave me a bad time in fourth grade. And I remember the war because we would get before the war. My grandmother sent most of our clothes to us, the material. Maybe the sleeves would arrive. Or in the next package, the rest of the garment. No duty today. Same with Fred's clothing. And then material, yardage. The last piece we had, a piece they called Bull Broad Club. And there was to be more, but there never was. And I became a freshman. And neither, excuse me, somebody's knocking on the door. And when I took track and sport and basketball and everything, well, in those days, we didn't wear shorts. I was unheard of. And we had bloomers, big bloomers that were painted all around. You've probably seen pictures. Well, I could show you myself in them. Maybe not in here, but I have them. And then, like a midi blouse, we used to call it. So mom used that last piece of material, made me this blouse. And it had elastic here and it bloused over. And to make the sleeves long enough, she had material from the side. It was wide material. So she just made extra piece for sleeves. And it had a little, what we call a Peter Pan collar, and it laced up the front. Wore that for years in all my sports. And after I was out of high school, then they got to regular shorts and different things for the girls.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there a lot of sports that you could take part in in high school?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Basketball, and I think we used a baseball when I was trying to play instead of softball. We played in the gym. We had to go from the schoolhouse downtown to the gym for all of our activities. And this, what they have up there today is wonderful. We should have had that years and years ago. In that gym, we held my mother and dad's golden anniversary in that gym. Over 200 people came. But it's just ideal as a complex today, I think there. And then I was in sports. I got my first gold medal. In fact, the first gold medal for Alcover in Orfino and low hurdles as a freshman. And the next year I got, I won five in track. I just walked away with everything in my long legs. Well, I didn't have any competition because a lot of girls were not interested. They didn't want to do this. And some of them couldn't. They can't coordinate in sports. Well, I excelled in sports. These other girls were better scholastically. But I didn't play any of those, any games like that when I came down here to college. Except tennis. And we played, we had two big tennis courts in Alcover. And some of us played tennis, some kids, not all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So would you say that there was quite a bit in sports available to women and girls if they wanted?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Yes. Even at women's basketball team.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you play on that?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: No, because I didn't live there as a woman. I left when I was 22. I didn't have one when I was teaching up there. No, I didn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the IWW? What do you remember about?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: All I can remember is these wobbly's we call them. The jail was across from us and right at the back door of the Morris's home. Of course, the Morris's didn't live there then. That was just an old creek bed with a bunch of old roots. The stumps were pulled out and they just dumped them over into that. But it was lots of fun to play all over those things. And they had these men in jail. One time we were there playing near the back and one of the men yelled out the window at Dennis Crasher. He was a cousin of the kids at Crasher's that live up there now. But he was my age and his sister was too. We were all close in age. And this man yelled and asked for niches. I said, Dennis, I think he wants matches. Don't get them for him. But what some of the things were, they did. I don't know, but I know they watched the mill closely. And I suppose they thought they would try to wreck something and bomb. Whether any of them were there, spies or not, I wouldn't know. Because they didn't speak English, they seemed very ignorant. They may have been very sharp. There were a lot of intelligent foreigners up there. And then as things increased and were better, more young people and young couples came in and they built more and more homes. That would have been from 1923 on through, well, the window closed. They moved lots of those homes out there in Moscow and in Pullman. They were here. People just took the whole house. They had some very nice homes up there at one time, especially that block where the homes stood up on the terraces. Elk River should not be in the condition it is today. There's no excuse for that, the plain laziness.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I see there were more Italians in families than there were Greeks. Well, Mr. Bacchus and Johnny Bacchus' father came from Greece. He married little Bedouin. They both died last year.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: And... De Mantis. Yes, but he had no family because he had a wife. Trying to think who else. Bacchus is a man, as far as family is concerned, but there used to be a lot of men around there who were Greek. And I don't know, let's see, if the Patishes were Servians or Yugoslavians. I think they were, yes. And they had one girl, Annie, Annie Patish.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is she still around?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: If so, I have no idea where. I'm sure her mother wouldn't be, but her father died. He was a... a hoot major too. There was a lot of that up there. Lots of it.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: And... Menelios or Antios were in the Italian family, the Bartons. Bartons used to have a store there, but it wasn't. It was in the business lot area, but it faced the school.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I can remember Mrs. Barton used to smoke cigars. And if somebody was like, I have to deliver meat to the back door, she'd put that cigar out before she'd open the door. Her daughters went to school, Irene went with me, and then she had a little sister like I did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that they were very much part of the social life?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: They were not. They had their own, like behind the Bartons, they had a long, long court.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you tell you that? They played bocce?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: No. And you could hear them all over town. They had a wonderful time.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Everybody in Germany or Europe knows all their country songs from the time they were little wee people until they died. Thank you. That's a wrap. We can make more too. So they had a quartet of these male voices. It was wonderful. Oh, Italian. I had an opera. They could sing all that operatic music too. Mr. Favreau lived up on the hill, and sometimes he'd get going. And you could hear him. From the hill where the Catholic Church was. You could just hear that voice all over that town. Beautiful. And they'd have programs lots of times for different things. And they'd usually sing at these shows and theaters, or whatever the program was. They gave a lot of plays to hometowns. And I don't remember who some of the other voices were among us at times, but they were real good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember what Andrew Bloom was like?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I just saw him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean, oh, you mean the father?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Yeah, the old man. Oh, yeah. He was a real nice guy. As far as I can say. He was very fine. As a boss, and handling his men, I think he was all right. I can't remember if they were hearing anything against him in any way. I know when we had programs and Fourth of July celebrations, he was always asked to speak. And he would walk back and forth. And it would be such a long speech. And he'd talk so slowly. He was not a young man when he went there to pursue all those duties as a superintendent. He had a very lovely wife and fine -climbed children. But his son is named Andrew, too. And I saw him when we were on our trip in Peninsula. He's a doctor in Auburn, Washington. I hadn't seen him, I guess, for 30 years, but I do correspond with his sister, Alice. And the other two girls live in California, too. So as we were going through with our friends, they were going to stop. He didn't allow people to stay around the town if they weren't going to be productive workers. He was really strong about that. I don't know. He had a man under him that was a Mr. Munson, I remember. And I can remember him telling men where there was work and to go. And especially when they had a big fire one time, he came along by the pool hall and asked him to fight fire.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, I was just like, can I hate to see them to go out to fight fire?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: But they can't say no. If they have to fight, they have to go fight fire.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you ever afraid of fires around Elford? That they might have to abandon town?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well, I got a little excited when there were two different times. They got the old shade trains ready with the flat cars to take the women and children off. But my mother never did. I don't know. She just had a lot of good common sense and foresight. And she never got excited, just like when that hotel burned in front of us. That was hot. Well, they turned the water on in front of the building. But she didn't bother to pull everything out of that front room. Not at all. She just had that much confidence because we couldn't go on. And another thing we had every summer up there for quite a few years was a town picnic. And you know, they had the logging trains and all this rail tracks all over in the woods. And they had one special place we went to for a picnic. I couldn't say how many years. They called Munson's Park, I think.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: You would go out of town just like the rail track was today. And then you turn off somewhere out around Bull Run and go up into the mountain. Well, not steep mountains there. I don't know how anyone ever found the place, but they would have swings and teeter -totters and a real nice picnic around out there. Everybody go on the old shade and go home on it. Take your own on share.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A lot of these town activities, how much did the single men participate in them?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: The sawmill workers, those people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they mingle freely with the townspeople in these kinds of activities?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: For that, they could. For something like that because it included the whole town. And as far as some of the other social life, well, no, I don't think they did when I was younger because there weren't enough women, for one thing. Not enough single women, in fact.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Let's see now, how was it?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Bowville had two men when I was a teenager and able to dance. Bowville had lots of girls, but an elk ever had a surplus of men. But to compare that with when I was younger and young people, the only young ones I can remember really are those that worked out in the woods. There were a lot in the mill, too. They didn't stay, and quite a few married women in Elk River and young women at that time, more like the lady that babysat me up here in the Archons restaurant. And I go to see her quite often. But she married a fella from Bowville.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: And old Bossy Snyder married another young woman that was there. I don't know whether she came in with her parents or not, but Eva did. Eva's father built that tall, two -story building, a pink one there in town. They had a bathroom, one of the few bathrooms in town. So she used to take care of Fred and me. I don't suppose we would even remember it, except she had a bathtub and she put us in the tub and gave us a bath.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: So, occasionally there'd be somebody who would marry a local girl who was still working in the woods. The mill workers. I think it was probably more from, actually, from the mill than the woods.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: There was a Mabel Munson. Her father was, I don't know whether he rated second to Mr. Bloom or not, but I know he was the boss. They lived in one of the better homes.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I was trying to think of some of the women, and Venus Snyder, the telephone operator.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I can't remember who she married. I tried to ask Eva some of these questions when I saw her Sunday. And she corresponded with Lena, who lives back in New York now. Well, until she became ill, that would be about five years ago, I guess. She still corresponded. And Lena's name didn't ring a bell to her, but maybe another time it will.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what about the sort of social activities that the adults were getting together in the early days? Would that just be a few families?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Dad used to win one after the other. See, they had dances because they danced above that store I told you about at one time before they ever had the gym.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well, there was a time, too, when quite a few people had horses. Worth back running. There were no cars early. But these social activities like pinochos and dancing for the adults, they would be mostly for the married couples. Most, yes. And in their homes. And the women had bridge clubs when they started playing auction. My mother had seven and eight tables of boots, and she'd entertain sometimes. She remodeled that house and made it an L -shaped living room, dining room.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would the Blooms and the other management people take part in those?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Oh, yes. Yes, they were all in that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the relation to Potlatch in town?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: It wasn't a company -owned town, exactly. Elk River was not. Potlatch was. And the competition in sports was very keen. Baseball and basketball. At least real keen. Even for the high school, for the boys, I never played basketball in the game. But I did in Elk River and Bowville and Fernwood and Dery. We had to travel on the train then. Nope, we didn't have automobiles. It stayed overnight. Then the train, the Milwaukee, went into Elk River. Then there was a branch line called the WINM into Bowville. And if you leave Elk River on the freight in the mornings, it would change to the WINM to go to Dery. And of course Fernwood and Clarky and Bowville were all on the same line. And we'd stay with the other players in their homes. And then when they come to our town to play with, they stayed with us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was basketball?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Yes, basketball. I always stayed with Edna Wade, her name is now. She lives here. She stayed with me from Dery. The same thing when we went to track in our field. That's how I met this friend that we toured the initial with, freshman in high school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you really made friends that way?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Made a lot of friends that way. And very wealthy with good friends. Really, my correspondence is just too much.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: From college, from grade school, from Elk River, and the college days. I was 31, I think, and was burned in the Africa, what they wanted out of. 32, I believe.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's been your understanding about why they shut the mail down?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well, I wish less of you were there like that. You could do better than I. They just felt that the larger mail here would handle everything at the time. And I think some of it was a change of... ...exavior of officers to younger people because they have regretted it. I feel it would have been far better off to have kept that mail, and this one too. And of course it was a big blow for all those people. They were out of work for a couple of years and they had no social security, not social security, but welfare or any way of getting any government help until many of them followed the mail here. But a lot of them had to wait until they were asked to come down here too and there was an opening. It was really rough on them. Very hard. That was the beginning of the Depression days. And we saw an awful lot of that in Oregon and California. We were lucky, less worked all through that. But I'm telling you, this is as bad as it is today with all the theft and the crime and watching every move you make. Be sure you lock your car and go out when you're in it.
