And when you got out of it, well that's a habit with me. I never ride with my car doors unlocked. And they are advised not to do that today too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did it do to your father's business?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Oh, it affected it greatly. It just dropped way down.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: He managed, they made a living for themselves there. But it did drop. And of course, thousands on the books, that was too lenient. That's where he and mom had problems. He didn't have to do that. They were the only butchering town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he get in trouble only after the mill went down, or was it before as well?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Then he had a lot of money on the books. Oh, I think it went on for years. Just leniency. Trusting people. Yeah, always. I can't find the records of it because mine might have burned all that stuff. Well, it's no different than a lot of stores. I mean, I've heard the same story from every small town about that period of time. Well, I'll turn you another thing that gives us the automobile. When they could get out of town and go to the chain stores.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But when they needed credit, could they get it there?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: No, no chain store would give you credit. You'd have to have your name on a book there to even cash a check. And I did a lot of it for the small businessman in any town. Now, a lot of people come down here from Arfino. Well, they're Arfino paper and Lecuski paper are sent to my husband complimentary because he has done a lot of work for him and helped him. And they appreciate it. Well, they're just very fond of him. They're young men, and they look at him as his age. Of course, I don't. My religion helps that way, too.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I read the ads in those papers, and a lot of things are cheaper than around here.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Yes, but not during the early years when he lost so much money because of the war and the rationing. Yes, he put in groceries and, oh, I don't know, before 1930, I guess, it started with a few things. Well, the others put in meats, cold lunch meats and things like that. So he just started doing some things gradually at a full grocery with it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that during the war, that during the wars, that there would be many people that wouldn't want to show up in his place because of his term?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I wouldn't say many in Elk River, no. But there were some, I'm sure. However, at that time, they had to because they had no choice. In the Second World War, there wasn't that same problem. They didn't burn up all the books and destroy things that were German in the Second War like they did in the First War.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So was there still a problem in the Second War?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: You mentioned before that there was some, the Second World War, there still was some. Yes, but I don't think they were affected at all. However, with me in Alameda, when we could send to our people, we learned that they were alive through the Red Cross, my brother got worried about them. And when we were allowed to, we started sending clothing, foods, and Dad sent lots and lots of food packages, and he cured bacon and ham himself for them. He had a smokehouse there.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: And, but with clothing, and with all the kindness, there wasn't anybody more helpful or more kind or generous in donating to anything than my folks at very Alco. I think I can just say that very truthfully. Far better than some of the rest. Not one person offered them a thing that they could send to the Loeb children or some of Dad's people, and there aren't a lot of them. But with me, my teacher friends just gave me loads of things to send in a fur coat. All kinds of things. And we brought a lot less, and I could well, well, money stretched quite a long ways then. And we, out of all that we sent, and Dad and my brother, only two packages were lost. 20 pounds was the limit. And it was surprising what you could get in that. And we bought shoes, best bought new shoes for all the little more babies. There were three, four, and six, seven at that time. A few more, a little bit later. And they, shoes was one thing they wanted, and you know how little kids love new shoes. And what school that they always show you is shoes. I usually wore a pump size, and I take off my shoe, and I said, I'll trade you. Let's trade shoes. You know, the first graders, they love that. But they remember that to this day when we've gone over there, Uncle Les. And the shoes went, I had been over there twice, and then in 70 he went with me, and then last year Uncle sent for us. Uncle Les, this and that, and they just sit all over and climb on him, these little people, because some of them are older now. They're married, but their little kids do. But they tell him that they remember that he sent their first shoes. Well, the only way they could really remember is the fact that it had been drilled into them, surely, for some of them. But it makes them feel good, and he'd buy these and take them out and scratch them on the walk to make them look used. And then they didn't have to pay duty. But I think we sent everybody shoes besides all his clothing, and our sister -in -law worked at Ward's, and she could get some very goodbyes for me there. And we'd ask them to tell them what they wanted, and sometimes they'd want something special in the sweater. They never asked for too much at all. Just what they needed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would this be starting after the war was over? Did they not want to let you do that before?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: No, it was after the war, yes. Yes. Because there was no way to mail it, and then they had that special rate for mailing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think they did that after the First World War II?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: If they did, I don't remember it. I don't. I just don't know. I never thought to ask my dad about that, for the First World War. But I know that he used to send packages of all his life, you know, all the time he was in business of something he could send to his mother. You know, as a kid growing up in Elk River, you didn't get out very much, I imagine. You didn't travel around. Well, I think we got a little more than some of them, because my mother's people were in Denver, and her mother and father, and she would take us there. We'd go by train, remember the first time. We had to sleep in a berth. Oh, Fred and I screamed and cried. That was in 1919. I was five years old, it would be about 1913, 1914. One car and one of the sleepers we had to sleep in one night was a wicker, all woven wicker. First, I remember that. Oh, Fred and I were terrible babies. My mother used to get so angry, she'd walk across the street to the store, and if she didn't find me in the house, she'd start screaming and crying and running after us. Well, you know, we had a fear of dad, too. And one time she was in the store, and she would get material, and I came along and plopped my hand on, and she had to buy out of all dirty.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Unofficially, I was hiding out from the immigration. I had worked in the spokesman review in Spokane, Washington, as a printer, but they had told me that after Christmas there wouldn't be too much work available, so I better ship for myself. I saw this ad in the union office that wanted a printer to tell who could tell the difference between poster type and calling card type, so I answered it. I rode all the way to Elk River, only to find out that Maranoy didn't want a kid, he wanted a printer. And I looked over his shop at his invitation, and I said, well, I can run everything but that thing that's sitting there. And it was a simplex type -setting machine, something that's unusual today. The Linotype Company gave him $400 when he bought his new Linotype, and he really bought the Linotype because I knew how to run one, and he didn't. But he knew how to run this simplex very well, and he got very much work out of it and a lot of proficiency considering the type of machine it was. And after the Linotype Company paid him $400 for it, they just took a hammer and broke it all up and just left it there. And really today it would be a museum piece, it would be wonderful to have one. I understand there is one around Derry someplace, in the barn.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The simplex machine?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: A simplex.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And where?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I don't know. But the one that was in Kendrick was sold to some farmer in the Derry area. And where it is, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you came out there to Elk River, what did you think of it? Were you real happy about going to work there from Arano at first?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well, yes, it was actually my second or third job that I had. I felt competent. I could hold, but at the time the man who left him wanted to come back. And when the immigration authorities came down there to pick me up, Marnold darned he told the immigration man that man would never become a public charge. He could stay with me as long as he wishes. And with that, the man went back to Spokane and reported in that I had a job I could support myself.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: But kind of an unusual story.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the deal now that you had immigration problems? And you were from Canada, but why were they looking for it?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well, this was kind of a confession, by the way. But what happened was that I came across the line and tried to pass immigration. And because I was 19, I only had approximately $50 in my pocket. And winter coming on, they thought that I would come down here and become a public charge. I was 19 years of age, and I told them I was a printer. And out of the three immigration officials, one of them just decided definitely that I couldn't be a printer. And there was no way to prove it. But it so happened that as I proved myself, I was. I've been printing ever since 1990. And I've never hardly been out of work, only between jobs. So did that mean they let you through, but not as a... No, they didn't let me through. They wanted to pay my way home. And I said, well, that's deportation. I won't take it. So I paid my way home. Back to Canada, yes. Then I met a very good friend of mine, acquaintance. We've been friendly all these years. And he told me he was going to Spokane. So I told him I would like to go to Spokane on a trip to see my brother who was there. And he said, well, you can ride along with me. It's all right with me. So I went down to Spokane. I got down and went down to Union office, and there was a job in the country at Colfax.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well, the man was an ardent Catholic, and they were burning Ku Klux Klan trees or crosses all over the place, which... Wasn't too good, because the man I worked for was really emblematic of what they were burning the crosses for.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Because he was Catholic?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: He was a Catholic.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You went into Colfax to take that job?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you working there for long?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I worked there a week, and then two guys would come there and sat beside me, and I didn't know who they were.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they told me, you see these crosses burning?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: And I said, yeah. And they said, well, that's for your boss. So I, in the meantime, just told them, well, let me finish up the week, and I'll get out of here. And I, a couple of days before that, I sat on the Colfax platform, wishing to go to Portland, because I had been a hockey player in Canada, and played in the championship of British Columbia the last year. And I was there, it was that year, you might say, and I thought I'd like to go to Portland and try out with the Rosebuds down there. I didn't think I'd ever make it, but I thought maybe I could get on some farm team that they knew about, because I didn't have a job. And there was only one place to work in the printing shop and trail outside of working in the smelter. And so anyway, I ended up by working for Marano and El Puro. I did go back and report to the immigration authorities and went through a thorough examination by them. I don't think they missed anything. But however, there was a man by the name of Hampton, who was the chief man, and he told me, he said, you got $8 in your pocket. And I said, yes, I got a lot more than that. And he said, well, take the $8, that's enough, go down and get a money order to pay your head tax. And then when he got through all that and accepted the money, or the money order, he said, now, young man, you're a pretty lucky boy. You could have spent some time in the penitentiary, you could have spent some time in a lot of places, and you could have been sent home. But he said, I advise you when you get back on your job, you go to the county seat the first available time you get and take your ship papers out. And that was the end of that. I've never had any more trouble with him. That was all cleared up that one time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, where did you learn how to do printing in the first place?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I learned my trade. I spent about four years between high school and time after high school. I spent four years and two months in trail learning the printing trade. I was taught by a very good printer, a man also by my name, but no relative at all. He was an Englishman, and he was a good printer. I wish today that I had collected all of his ability. And I worked in trail that time, and things become unsatisfactory there, and that's my move to the United States. I did, however, try to get other jobs around there, but nothing were available.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say unsatisfactory, is that wages weren't good enough?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well, I was getting a handsome price of $22 .50 for 48 hours' work. Now, that's a long time ago. I went to work for Marano, and I got $32 .50 a week for the like hour. So it was just like getting a good raise and pay. Ten dollars was quite a lot of money in those days. And from there on, I worked for Marano for about five and a half years. I also ran and edited his paper while he was in Idaho's legislature. He spent two sessions down there, and each time I produced his paper.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I got by with it. I got by. That's about all I can say.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I learned about learning the trade in trail.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did the guy teach you? How did you learn the training in those days?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Hand -setting type.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he show you how to expect you to know how to do it? In trail?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Oh, no, I worked. I learned how to feed press, and I learned how to set type.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he teach you how to do it?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: The first two years under that Englishman, I'm telling you something. I think I learned it by the mallet. I think he pounded it into me. But he was good to me. You could work with tears in your eyes all day. At the end of the day, he'd say, good work today, kid. Good work today. He was a character, but oh, what a printer. Then I learned more from an apprentice that had gone there. I had learned his trade there. Then he had gone to Spokane, worked over in Tacoma, and then he came back to trail and bought an interesting with a man by the name of Hall that owned the controlling engine. When I say satisfactory, along with everything, when I left the shop, this bully left too. He dissolved partnership. It took him a little time to get everything settled, but he quit the same time I did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the method when you say you could be working with tears in your eyes, and the method of teaching sounds maybe a little bit harsh?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: The Englishman was that way. They were very stern.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Not that he was actually unkind or anything, but he was just to the point. He just pushed me and learned. I think I learned the way he learned. The old method in England was so much alike, that type of apprenticeship. He pounded into me. He used to tell me, you do it right, set it fast, work hard. That's about all I did. I fed that old press up there. In fact, I fed all their presses. The paper came along, and I had the sad ads. I never got a chance to learn the line of type while he was around. However, one time the partner that was sick went to Hot Springs up there. I don't know what Hot Springs was, but he had rheumatism so bad he couldn't work. The old Joe Clark took to running the line of type, and he changed some things.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: He just couldn't get the slugs to come out of the line of type machine. Every time he'd come around to the ejection part of the machine, it would stall. I was speeding press, and he went out to lunch. I knew what was wrong, so I went down and changed it, and didn't say anything. He came back and tried to run again. Sure enough, the slug came out like it should. He just had it on the wrong size. He looked at it, and then he stands up and he said, kid, come over here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do with this machine?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I told him I changed that from 8 point to 10 point, so he'd get the 10 point slug out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, haven't I told you never to touch this machine?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: You see, that's what I mean by tears in your eyes. I think I have a picture of that shop.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I got one on trail.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Those were regular pack horses.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well, he didn't do the packing, but he did.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: This was a store. On the other side, this was a hotel. We had more pictures of these. Oh, that was a factory.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There's the old clock house down there, see?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: And this area had been burned over. There's a cabin called the Bean cabin.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you hike up to Elkview?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I never go up there. I'd been there once, but only hiked up the last park. I never hiked from town up there. I'd been halfway several times. The one trick was to climb up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the kids use the go to Elkview in the fall?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: All the time. We swam down there in the summer. A lot. There were two very good swimming pools there. Oh, yes. That was another good picnic and outing place. Of course, we could walk it four miles until we had cars to go in. That's the clock house that I showed him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of Elk River, the town, as somebody knew coming in?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: To me, it was a lark. I was used to a town of about 7 ,500 population. Gosh, it was a surprise to me that everybody knew everybody around there. Going into the pool halls was a kick because those guys had worked down in the sawmill or the office of the mill or something like that. Then they would really take it out on each other either before work or after work. They were always gibbetsing. Gibbetsing, giving each other a hard time. Oh, yeah. They'd ride a guy to death around here. They really worked on it. It was interesting to me. I was told by the Union Secretary. It was Secretary of the Typographical Union.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why shouldn't you stay in a town like that?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well, he figured it was too small for me. Your printing experience comes better with metropolitan experience. Much better. Well, you just learn more efficiency. When you hit the big time, you are pushing a little bit harder and you know how. Experience, of course. A lot of times I relied back on the very fundamentals I learned in country shops. It was good that way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have much time to spend with the local guys? Was your job free enough that you had time to kill and go to the pool hall?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Oh, not doing working hours. Not doing working hours. No, I work from eight to five every day. Well, you played basketball. I learned how to play basketball there. I played baseball. I went skating one day when I had some ice around there. Gosh. It was funny. I was down there, you know, and having skated well in my life, and put on a pair of old skates and started going around that millpawn around there. People come down the roads as far as the community was concerned to see what was going on. And I think Marie will vouch for you. I could skate there. She mentioned that before. I believe it. Well, you were here, so I got ahead of myself, I guess. I believe it. But it's been a good life for me. I can't complain. Maybe I would have done better if I had stayed in Canada and played hockey.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you feel that you were coming down here because the opportunities were better for working?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: No, I had to make a living. I didn't have a job on trail. That answers the question. You came because you had to support yourself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you think you were going to want to stay down here after you've been here for a while?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well, there was one circumstance about it. I had to stay for a year because Maryknoll guaranteed me a job for a year. So I had to stay there, and otherwise the immigration good thing had sent me home. And I didn't have a job on trail, although they were very anxious to have me come back and play hockey, as I understand the story. But I never went back. Never to play hockey. Five years later, or five and a half years later, I played a couple games in Spokane one winter. And I got a letter from Bobby Rowe of the Portland Rosebudge, or Buckaroos rather, wanting me to come down for a tryout. And that was just about the time the depression started. So that ended having a big crew. However, he did mention the fact he wanted me to go up to his farm team in Canada and try out with them, go play hockey up there. And he said, Rossland, B .C., and then hit seven miles from trail, and that would be professional suicide or hockey suicide to go play against your hometown. So I wouldn't go there. He wanted me to go up maybe to Salmon Arm District or maybe over in the prairie or something like that. It might have been different, but to go to Rossland and play against trail, it wouldn't have been too healthy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the championship team that you played on? Do you remember that?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Oh, it was just a team. We were defeated by a team by the Enderby, British Columbia, for the championship. We lost out in two games. We had to play two games and count the goals. And the first game ended five to four. On the second game, they won two to one. And we literally played in water because the season was just about over. Well, they didn't have much choice. We played on natural ice.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were in Elk River, did you get much riding because you were Canadian? Did they review about it?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Oh, I used to get a little bit once in a while and get talking out of turn or something like that. And of course, a lot of them kind of made fun of me because I talked so funny. I guess some of my expressions were different from the Scandinavians up there, for sure. But it was interesting. I can't complain. One thing I'd like to ask you about is...

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Now, this is actually jumping ahead a little bit. It was after you left because you left in 30.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you understand about Mironot's moving to Moscow and working with Robinson?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well, I think it was a very good opportunity for Mironot. He was just about at the end of the road in Elk River as far as running a paper in Wisconsin. And I think his movement there was good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, whose idea was to start a newspaper?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I don't know. But Mironot was a good job man and a good publisher for the size of the town he was from. But to enter into Moscow, which was a much bigger field, it was a challenge for him. However, the doctor had money and they hired the editor from the Boaz from the other paper, so there wasn't too much. Yeah, there wasn't too much. I read in the way Robinson told the story that Mironot came to Moscow one day and dropped in to see him and said, I'd be interested in doing some of your printing. And he quoted him a price which would have been about half what the star mirror was charging. And so he decided to go with it. Well, I'll tell you something about Mironot. If he depended on the printing he got in Elk River, he wouldn't even make a living. We were so busy printing job work for the Pyle H. Lumber Company, for other people, for other towns, and the different areas around there that we were just busy all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Mironot would leave the shop and go out maybe three or four days and come back with a hat full of work. And boy, we really worked. He did work for the Pottlatch, W .I. and Am Railroad, and Bovo, Clarkia, Fernwood, those lumber companies, and Fernwood would even send him job work. He was aggressive. About getting work. Yes. He was on the ball, I would say. Well, you do it. You ought to do it too because you had a dependable printer to get it out. You can afford to be out with you if you're alone. You need your shop to do those things. You lose time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that make, do you think that made a lot better living for the paper than the shop that the newspaper did?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: All of the newspapers, only a two -day workup. Now, Mironot would write the paper, and the first day I would set it on these new linotype he got. And then on the following day, I would make up the paper, set the ads and everything, and Mironot would write up a few more things, and he in turn was learning how to run the linotype too. So I would make up the paper, and he would make it up along with it, whatever he wished, and also make the corrections on the galley proofs. And we worked together all the way through. And one thing I'll say about Mironot, that when he started that paper on the Wednesday morning, before we went home Thursday night, that thing was all printed, folded, and sometimes mailed. And that paper is usually always in the mail Friday morning before nine o 'clock, and that was the day of publication. And he never failed. Only time he ever fails when the press broke down on time. He made some innovations up there for sinking of the floor, and the result was that one of the contraptions he made, the nut flew off the bolt and went into the gear and wrecked the press.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Oh, we had our troubles up there.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well, I would say this, Mironot paid for his shop. He bought a home. But you didn't pay it on the newspaper alone. No, it was the job work that paid it. But I think the newspaper paid him. We only spent on the average of two days on it. And I got the handsome price of about six dollars a day or so. And that was pretty good money, however, in those days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that job work was what would make the difference for our first home newspaper?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Definitely. And Mironot, by the way, had good job printing equipment. Good job printing equipment for a country shop. In fact, if he hadn't had such a good shop, I think I would have been long gone. But he had a good shop.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How would he come buy that?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: That was before you purchased that from the previous... I understand that a man by the name of Durslin ran the paper in Elk River and put the shop in there. And during the war, he kind of folded up on it. Then he left Elk River, closed the publication, left Elk River, and went up around Mullen and started another shop in the mining area. And Mironot come along with a man by the name of Tony Hooner. And they bought it together. But Tony Hooner only stayed there a short while. And Mironot stayed on. And he hired printers all the time. And he paid them going wages. But I think Mironot's progress increased after he bought the Linotype.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did the Linotype mean in the way of progress? What did that make it possible for him to do?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well, he spent a lot of time on that simplex typesetting machine. That was... Speed, for one thing. With the type on the Linotype, you could get about a gallon, quarter -gallon, half an hour. And we only had about, oh, maybe 10 or 12 gallons of type So that only lasted for one day. Whereas the other way, well, he was always on that simplex. And half the time it would run. And, you know, and all the simplex letters all had the combination on the back of it. It's all movable type. And it all had these combinations. And you put that on the top. And it would go around. And it would finally find its combination and drop in there. I don't know how many channels there were in that thing. It was a different kind of a keyboard. I wonder why he got the simplex instead of the Linotype in the first place. Well, it was there. Oh, that's right. He bought the old equipment. That's a wonderful picture. I didn't realize how much it was in that. Excuse me. It really is. Well, it was the bank. I didn't realize it. It was the big bank. Well, they were just playing on a Sunday afternoon. They had a man there by the name of Sibitz. He was a Spanish -American war veteran and a band leader. And he was there, but he had died before I got to town. And he led the band. And a marinot took it over. But Sibitz was a band master. There was no doubt about it. They were learning from him where Bill could maybe play saxophone and clarinet. And it was a little handicapped.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well, he was an outright musician.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that what he did?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: He was retired, but he would do music. He could teach music. And he could make a little living doing that. And you probably had that tension in Sibitz. I never had an opportunity to meet him because he was gone before I got there. But all the boys around, like Bill Vavly and Norman Vavly and all those fellas, they just thought the world of them. And he knew his music and he had several good musicians there. And then they had this guy, Sam Grandy, here, who learned how to play the clarinet in Italy, I guess. And of course, that's just like a wet -breed stick to those guys. They keep blowing. His wife lives here. And Mrs. Grandy lives here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How can they learn shoes?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: He was the shoemaker in Elk River. He sold shoes and repaired them. His name is Josh. I think he should be a good person for me to talk to. I haven't really wanted to talk to him to some.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: There were characters in their own ride. And then we had some...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Pavich, what was his name?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Servian. Servian. He was a hotel man and a bootlegger, you might say. He was accused of it anyway. And they had their clan together that way. They pretty much ran with their own group all the time. Well, he had their pool ball in a hotel and one of the dressers of the same nationality would gather there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A pabbage?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: A pabbage. But I don't know afterwards, years later, but I don't think they had it too long. Several people managed it. Colletto was so surprised that I produced a couple pictures that proved to her that people by the name of Randall had it. Because I was afraid of their daughter Loyan and they were going to have her picture taken and they took me with them to the Japanese photographer that's downstairs. I didn't bring it up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And my picture too, when my mother was so mad, she said, well, why didn't you tell me I would have fixed your hair?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: It's not bad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that that picture of you that's here? Is this your picture?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Yeah, that's not supposed to be up here. I guess it was on the, it mixed in, that was me. When I was teaching school. Oh, from Moscow. 20 years old.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: That's a nice picture. It's a nice picture. We had a... Well, not especially, but it kind of marked me, I guess. Many people tell me about it. This has a picture he insists it's up all the time, like in the bedroom. I put it away once and he said, that's to stay on. That's the way he remembers me in alakai, that he has to look after me. I don't know. You didn't tell him the first time you saw me, I thought I was the homeliest looking guy. No, I didn't get around to that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that the truth?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: That's what she told the rest of the girls. Well, it must have been a sign she was affected by you. No, I don't think it was that at all. I had trained all summer to get in shape for a plain ice hut. And I was really in a wonderful condition. But my plans got changed and I come down there skinny. I think I had about... One of mine too. One of his boys had a 19. Well, I was big enough to hold my own with men up there. That's right. But I think I had a 36 inch chest, a 31 inch waist. And I fattened up after I got to eat and write better up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the beer?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: No, it didn't have much beer up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you make up for it in California?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: No. I remember very well when I decided to get married here. Oh, that's another story. I went together five years. I was a senior, I guess, when I really started dating more steadily. And then I came to school. I went to school for years. Well, so nice to go to school and be able to support myself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know that you wanted to teach for a long time?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well, yes. I went elsewhere for a girl most days. A nurse, a student, a little teacher. It's been a long choice you had. And I certainly didn't want to be a slave to a typewriter. And she certainly didn't want to get married in El Cervé. I got married in El Cervé. I mean to say, you didn't want to stay if you got married. You decided you didn't want to stay there. So I married the only guy that wasn't fallen for war. Well, the only guy didn't work in the sawmill. Well, I wondered if she wanted to go someplace different. Well, I think it was my old mother. I don't know, especially. My mother used to say, well, don't worry about what people say. You're not going to be in this town all your life. She encouraged me a lot. And my folks wanted me to go to school. I definitely didn't. I just didn't know how you would be saved. Really. That's what they expected of me and I didn't. Then this comes along and it's a lot better than many guys. You can say no you need to go to school. I don't want you to. It was very much in favor of you. And then I went to the California and I had to go to school to get my degree.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I mean a lot of women probably didn't do any of those three things, right?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: They just didn't, you know, quite a few women didn't want a career at all. Well, you didn't care too much about it, frankly. You could get married in Elk River. And I was the first girl to go out to school from Elk River to this Mary. And well, some had moved away too, but I don't know if any one of them ever went to school either. We were the fourth class and there were only two boys in the first and there were four in the second. And two girls are three boys and the third one, I think. The girl that graduated with me went on to nursing, but right away I thought she did, but she tells me no. And she didn't become an RN, however she owned several restaurants, small ones like we used to have in large homes. And from then on, let's see, after I graduated, Joy would be the one from her class, a Fred's class. I think, oh, in the old one, two girls followed right here at normal school. They both were teachers, too. Yes, and they became teachers. And one of those was passed away since. And from then on, I don't remember, but I know every year somebody has continued on in college from Elk River High School. They never had a class over 16 graduate, I don't believe. And we made, Elk River became a credit to their 19th. Twenty -four. And for the county historical society. I didn't work for the paper. That was the other guy that was there. I worked for the historical society.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Five years, was that an unusually long courtship for those days?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I don't know. I don't think so. Of course, I have to look at Elk River because every young woman that came to Elk River to become the secretary for the country, found herself a man pretty fast.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Isn't that right?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Every one of them married. And I can remember right now. Well, those days they didn't like the time. And not many of the school teachers that came there married. Well, the school teachers. Well, quite a few, but there's an awful lot. And I think way back, they didn't. One time, some young fella and an older man came to Elk River to repair the turbines that were making the electricity for Elk River. They become used up and needed repair. And my gosh, this one guy come to town here and he did a courtship in the fast way. I think he came there in September, October. The first year they were through, so he took the first grade teacher with him. And I don't know whatever, become him. His name was Raw, R -A -U. I just forget her name. My gosh, I don't even remember that. They got married right away and took off. But he was a very good mechanic. And he was under this older man, who was evidently an expert. He came from Philadelphia after working in Elk River.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember that?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well, you went there then. You stayed in Elk River the whole time that you were in school, teaching school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were still working in Elk River?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Oh, yeah. Planning to marry eventually. And my ring, the year after I was down here, I guess. Well, the last year, the last year, I, well, she taught two years there. I took off in November to go to the coast. 1929.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were teaching here? Oh, and you were teaching here after?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well, I took off to go to the coast to play hockey and didn't make the grade. And I went back and got my stuff and went back there and played amateur hockey. Sort of a league they built up there. They were all good players, some very good players. And it was sort of a league formed by the businessmen for the interest of the hockey players that stayed in town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where was it?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: In Portland. We played every Sunday night. And I played there and I got this job. Of course, the depression was right on then. You couldn't even get a job. And I played for this outfit one night and we had a tie game and they hadn't been winning, I don't think at all. And the next night we played at the Mount Noma Club and we beat them five to nothing. And that was after I'd had a couple of workouts with these boys. And the next day I was leaving town and the boy at the Goodsell couldn't see that at all. And I ended up by working in the garage all winter. And I worked there all the time. I didn't do much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You said the Goodsell? What's Goodsell for it?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Oh, Goodsell for Icy. They were the team you were playing for. We sponsored. We finished up on Sunday night. And the next day I got a job out in Oregon City in the print shop. But you had worked in a job shop in Portland because you printed our announcements. Oh yes, I worked as a line type operator for a while. A small place there, but he didn't have too much work. He just had this one book to get out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What made you decide to leave Elk River at that time? Well, I always had a feeling, just like I told, who was that outfit that came there and bought out at actually store?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Wilkie Morgan. Wilkie Morgan. They came to the shop and they wanted to know whether Elk River was a good place to live. And I had to tell them naturally that it was a good place to live and everything. And then they got to talking more or less confidential to me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said, you see that sawmill down there?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: And I said, yes. I said, well, that's like a horse. If it drops dead, there's going to be nothing here. And they kind of thought I was a little bit harsh about it. But that's actually what happened. They bought the store and they stayed and they left too. And they closed up the store. But I had lived so many places in Canada, in the mining company and country up there, like Weimer and Prudeville and all the rest of them around there and heard of the different communities. And one industry town has no place to stay. Not for me. I'm surprised now. I even stayed in Elk River because of the fact if I had a radio thought back, I never had any ties or property or anything. All I did was buy automobiles. I had a new one on every time I could tell. You're not telling the right story.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you mean?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: They only sent it for 500 bills.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, yeah?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: They took that many.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They did?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: They took 500. The last one landed there, though.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: But I've seen those mining camps, logging camps, one after the other just become nothing overnight.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you suspect that that might happen in Elk River? Did it look shaky to you?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: No, it looked good. It looked good. And Marano always used to emphasize the fact there was enough timber up there for 80 years in Elk River. In fact, before the Potlatch Lumber Company was formed, it was just the Potlatch was up there. But I mean the main company now was called the Potlatch Lumber Company. At one time, they were going to come to Elk River and bring all that timber that come down here. They were going to bring it into Elk River. And I think they would have been ahead of the game had they done that and build another complex up here around Weyper. So they learned about it and learned that Elk River might be increasing. He was just really moving around town to find out where he could buy a lot so he could build a shop. Marano was a cautious man. He didn't want competition. And he wanted to be there first, you might say, with the most so nobody would come in. And he was all excited about it. In fact, in Elk River, they had gone up the hill one place and graded more on the street in hopes that they would have locations to build more homes there should such a thing come in. But for some reason or other, it was all decided to come down here. That was prior to the decision to put that mill in here at Lewiston. Probably when they were trying to decide what to do, that idea came up. I think the idea must have grown. Of course, in Elk River, they had an awful lot of snow to move. And that was, I think, part of the drawback. And another drawback, I think, I've heard this story, that they had to sign another agreement with Chicago, Milwaukee, and St. Paul Railway. See, the 20 years had practically run out with the contract with the Milwaukee Railroad. And they had to make a new agreement.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: 20 years contract for what? For hauling?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: For hauling all manufactured lumber. All the lumber had to go out of Elk River on that 20 miles of road to Boulder. Everything. Elk River was shipping a lot back east, and those freight cars were loaded. They used to take anywhere from 8 to 12 cars out of there every day. And it was good lumber. It was well graded lumber. They had a good white pine stand. They had a bunch of good graders up there at the end.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So how did this affect their decision?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well, I think that the Milwaukee wanted to negotiate a new contract, or new freight rates, or a new hauling deal, or something like that. And I don't think, not sure, but I don't think the potlatch was going to go for it, because maybe they were up in their rates. Because things had changed, you know, as far as rates were concerned.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: And whatever happened, the decision was made by all the warehouses interested. And I think that they, well they used to go out to, around Breakfast Creek, and stay out there, and camp out there in the summer. And I think they got the idea of floating the logs down, and everything would come from that kind of visitor.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you mentioned when we were talking before that it was the younger generation, you thought, that was, didn't you say that?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well, the younger ones that were more interested in the mill. The executives of the mill kind of pushed that, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Isn't that one of the stories, too?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: They had different views on this, and they were old college men, graduates.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do things happen today? The youth or sons take over, or is there a vice president's position for him in the company?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I hadn't thought of telling him. I guess about every year around here. That's what I needed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you got out just before it happened, didn't you?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I mean a year or two. Well, what I did, I walked right into the stock crash in Portland. That's actually what I did. When that stock crash came about there, they just laid off help right and left. I think Wes had been out there in it like that, had a taste of it. He was reluctant to get married, a little train of sort. It hit hard in Portland right away. Oh, yeah. Oh, the papers, really. You know, I was a printer, and the papers and the job shops and everything. They just laid off men all the time. I met a regular on the newspaper, and he learned I was a printer. He said, boy, I'm sure sorry for the subs on the board, because they just didn't hire them at all. They just cut down the paper, the advertising cut down. I know Goodsell, he wanted to get me a job in a print shop, you know, and he'd call up a guy and say, well, never order another sheet of paper from him, you know, to hire this guy. He was the guy that carried it himself. Portland was hard hit with stock crash.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you stay and manage to make a living in Portland?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well, as I said, I played hockey for Goodsell all winter, and he hired me in his garage to keep me there. I don't know where I would have gone, but to look for a job. But when we beat the Multnomah Club that night, I was leaving town the next day. I had to get some kind of a job.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you get married long after that?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I got married in May. That was in the stock crashes sometime in October and November. I played all winter, and then I come back in May and married her. I was on a job, but it was somebody's job for me. He was going to Oregon State. It was his job for the summer, so I was kind of out. So I ended up in Springfield after we were married. I worked in Springfield for a man by the name of Maxi that owned the Springfield paper. I've forgotten the name of it. I also would work occasionally for the Eugene Register, and it went under. It went under. It's now the Register Guard. There were two papers in Eugene. They combined. They let some of them off, or must have out, too. They had a steady job at the Register. I decided to go to lineotype school and went to little money I had and left. Bill Nairnow's sponsorship and arranged for him to go to school. Through Bill and getting a machine there, and also one of the lineotype representatives was pretty handy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I remember the name of Bowman, wasn't it?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Anyway, I went to school down there and studied lineotype. From there, I went on to work in it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you aware of what was going on in Moscow when they had the battle between the two newspapers, between the news review and Star Mirror?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: No. I was down in California that time. There was a battle. I still think if the old man that owned the Star Mirror hadn't met with that accident, I still think that he would have put up a good fight. But you see, he was killed with a gunshot in an automobile. They were hunting or something. He met with this accident and that just disturbed everything. He was trying to get Robinson pretty bad. Prior to his death, I guess he was the one that initiated proceedings to try to deport him from Robinson.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where was Robinson from to be deported?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well, the... He didn't change his name. Robinson is religionless. I've never read his book or any of his information at all. No, he used to advertise the man who talked with God. That's kind of a hypothetical way to attract attention anyway because...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could he prove it?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well, what do you think Mr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Maranov thought of it? Do you think that he thought he was sincere?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well, Maranov is a very sincere Catholic.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: But Bill said there's a lot of printing, but he sells it. That's what he told me. He says he sells this stuff all over. Gets big money for it from some people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean, do you think that he thought that Robinson really believed it? That Robinson believed in what he was saying?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well... I wouldn't know how to answer that because Bill says, well, he sells this printing, people read it, and send it all over the world. Some people make large contributions to him. See, that's what a number of people have said to me that they didn't think that Robinson was really sincere, that he himself believed what he was saying. Which I find a little hard to believe myself because he was writing all that stuff all the time, and I would think he must believe it. I don't see how he had the time. He worked as a druggist or a pharmacist, and I don't know where he got the time to write all this stuff to get so... Once he got going, I think he used to vote himself full time to it by the late 1930s or early 40s. I think he was pretty much doing that all the time. But he wasn't just writing, he was traveling all around his speech. He must have had somebody working for him too. Oh, he had a big staff of mostly women sending out those mailings. I think he must have employed at least 30 people in the mosque, but it wasn't the biggest employers in the town in the mid -30s. The way they were set up, they couldn't touch the legal. They needed a legal rating. So they went over to Troy and purchased the Troy News. And that's how it was called the News Review. Because the legal aspect came in there and they'd had to print for 78 weeks under the Idaho Code to be eligible for a legal rating. But by purchasing the title to the News in Troy, which was already a legal paper, they themselves gained a legal rating and could print legals now. There might have been a little controversy between Lamphere and the courthouse and other politicians and attorneys. And I think that's how their success came about. Now, if they hadn't bought that Troy News, they'd have suffered for 78 weeks without a legal rating.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And that's what stops a lot of papers today from getting started in Idaho, is it 78 weeks? What's California made of glass?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: One year. And Washington is six months.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I don't know how it goes in other states, but those three states I am acquainted with the laws. When I owned my paper down in Nevada City, I had a little legal entanglement. The opposition in Grass Valley said that I didn't have a legal rating. And I had a legal rating and a judgment from the court. There was a judge to the legal paper, but for some reason or other, the opposition was able to stir up a little propaganda that I actually didn't have a legal rating. I beat them four times down there. And four times they had an election and they were beaten each time. Then they were coming up for the fifth, but a fella came in and wanted to buy the paper. Right now. And he had the money. And so he wanted to take it right now, and I said, oh, no, no, we've got to go to the court, or up to the attorney. And I said, I've got to run a notice in the paper that I'm disposing of the business and notice the creditors and so forth. They're selling out. They have a law like that in California. And it takes ten days for that to go through. Then he wanted the editorial policy anyway, so I'll throw that in. So anyway, I sold out. It was a controversial area. I was a flatlander, and Hillbillies kind of didn't like it. Everybody in town was related somewhere, somewhere.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In Nevada City?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Yeah, in the Gaspel and Nevada City. Well, all that gold country is like that. So it's growing out of it now because the younger people that are the older generations today don't stay there so much. But you were opposed to the consolidation because it would rob the community. I told them it would. I said, you'll lose your businesses. I said, the kids will go over to Grass Valley and the store over there will show some new shoes or some new sport clothes or something. And everybody will be rushing over there to buy them all the kids and be exposed to it. The school was to be in Grass Valley, and that would put them down. And that's exactly what happened. They believed me after it happened. But I still don't know whether I sold that business to the man that bought it or to the high school. I'm still a month down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think it's more difficult than, say, around here for people to make the right decisions? You know what I mean?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Maybe you don't. I'm thinking of, heck, it's all -day problems around here, too. Even thinking about whether schools should go together or not. Well, they have the same thing up in Troy and Derry. They fight that out every weekend, I think. And it will kill if they make a union high school out of that, or it's going to kill one town. Well, Bogle's already killed. Yeah, Bogle's dead now. And if Elk River is swallowed up by Orifino, there just won't be anything in Elk River. I don't think that Elk River will allow it. I think they'll fight, as they said, up there. They'll fight it. Like my attorney tells me now, he says, people just come in and say, understand they like you. He told you that when you were in the meeting. That's right. People were just commencing to like you and understand what you meant when you were fighting this school. But I couldn't convince them in four times that we had beaten the law. But the funny thing about it is, all these high school contests were an election. They can call one every six months, and they can go till they win. And then there's no recourse. You can't go back. And that's the hell of that law. It's just wrong. And that city was four miles from Grass Valley, which had a date, and they were sure worried about us. It wasn't good. It wasn't worried. You never would have gone up there into that competition if you hadn't been. And so when the high school was already voted to go together, I wouldn't ever have bought in Nevada City. But I didn't know it. Lo and behold, three weeks after I got there, I found out a lot of things. But they could never get the funds to build. And that's the very thing that I stopped there for four years, or practically four years. And oh, I'll tell you something. Every week was different. I liked the climate and living there, but I sure didn't like a lot of the people. People I was in contact with school. It was wonderful. I couldn't ask any better. I got wonderful breaks all through my teaching. But business -wise, it was quite a different story. Well, I think the high school was intimidating everybody that advertised with me. I think that was one of their policies. If they took an ad with me, they would get intimidated by the Nevada Union high school system. And it hurt. Really, a lot of it hurt. I could have gone along with the thing, but I couldn't see going in debt in the county for 30 years, which they've done now, and they don't know where they're coming out. Of course, they've had a lot of people come up there, but taxes have gone out of sight. And they come up there and stay till they find out they don't like it, and they sell out and leave. It's not a good area to retire in. Oh, yes. I thought so at the time we were there. They didn't want to stay. They all left. But now, when we get some little paper from there, it isn't the one that we had. And there seems to be more youth, more young people. Well, I wanted to ask you a little bit about the church activities in Elk River when you were there and growing up, and what the Union church was like. Well, I think it was very nice. I think it's a very pretty little church. And one of the pictures that we saw was that some people were showing. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When do I never appreciated it anymore than I did with that film?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: And I think it had a lot to do with the center of activity in Elk River because they used to have, for adults, boxers, socials, and entertainment in that basement. The place was really well planned for the activities needed with the church, which included anybody that wanted to come. And, of course, we always went to Sunday school. A friend and I didn't go to church very much. You know, years ago, your ministers, to me, that scared me to death, listened to them up there harking and talks screaming. I always thought, well, gee, I've been doing the wrong thing all the time, writing me. So I went to Sunday school. I tried to teach Sunday school because it's hard to get anybody to do it. But today they really have a good method for it. And I'd say it was quite active. And then the Catholic Church, we had, there wasn't as large, a congregation wasn't, although they could have had, if they had had priests that would come down to earth and talk to all those Italians. But they seemed to frighten so many of these single men.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean the single people didn't have much to do with the Catholic Church?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: That's right. And there were all those, most, all those foreigners were Catholics, but they didn't attend. I don't know of any that did, but maybe they contributed. I don't know about that. It was the same way in potlatch. The Catholic Church did not draw the foreign population. And then they had another church started there in Nazarene, and that did fair for those who really liked that church. They were faithful to it. And it was held first in the upstairs of that store I showed you. Well, one of the biggest mistakes that the church made in Elk River, for some reason or other, the guy that owned the theater was doing a little bit of bootlegging on the side. And for some reason or other, they got a going that they couldn't run shows on Sunday up there. Elk River had very little entertainment. These are a pool hall. Like the other dorks said, you don't want to go to Elk River. Heck, all they got is a pool hall to go to. There's only four of them. And they had four or five pool halls. And my gosh, if they didn't pass a city ordinance that you couldn't have shows on Sunday. And I think that hurt the church pretty badly. It was the young men anyway. They took pretty mean to it. Then everybody used to get in the car and go to Boville Sunday show. Boville always had, I had quite a show there, and Old Man Den of them, I guess. Oh, yes, absolutely. Old Man Den of them ran that show down there. He used to advertise all the time on Elk River, you know, and everybody would go to Boville to the show on Sunday. And the church really made a mistake, I think, by doing that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the movie theater and Elk River shut down all together there?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Oh, no. It stayed there. No, but it couldn't run on Sunday. Then finally they got it opened up. They could go on Sunday afternoon. They even tried to stop the baseball on Sunday, but that didn't go there. I'll tell you, they had some people that were a little bit too overboard. It wasn't going to drive men to church, for sure. It would drive them out of church. But we had an element there that was against Sunday shows and Sunday baseball.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think they might have been more strong in that direction because they didn't like bootlegging and all that stuff?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I know there was a lot of that that went on around the object. I thought that might make more religious people to go overboard. Well, this one particular man, friend, was the leader of it. And he was the one that was running around with the petition to stop Sunday shows. And they got sufficient voters over this controversy. The man was bootlegging and so forth. But it didn't build a church up any at all. I don't know what he was trying to do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he the Reverend in the church?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: No, he was the prohibitionist. He was a good Democrat, but he was the prohibitionist. Speaking of Southerners, some people in the Elk River have talked to me about the Klan being in Elk River too.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: A couple people have said to me that they thought they were mostly Southerners that were in the Klan during that time. No, no. I'm pretty sure that friend, the man we're talking about, he was a Southerner more or less from down there. He was in the Klan. And of course, I don't know for sure, but I'm sure that Mandy Page was one of the good clusters up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he a deputy sheriff or not?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: He was a deputy sheriff.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he a Southerner too?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: No, but he was... In fact, they lived out here in Nesperce, where they came to Elk River from. And his daughter was my teacher too. But the Klan never was too strong up there. And I don't think they scared anybody. I think it was just wishful thinking that they burned a few crosses around there. I don't think they burned any more than about two or three at the most. So they didn't really try to do anything so far as you knew.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They didn't intimidate anybody here?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: No, no. I think it was just a significant symbol of the Ku Klux Klan. The others were around.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: But my first deal, like I said, was over there in Ku Klux, and they were really burned around. Of course, Ku Klux was kind of Catholic town. They had that school over there, and they had that nunnery or whatever they called it. They had the whole thing pretty well working. I guess it's still there. But I know when I was working for this place, it called fact commoner than the nuns would communication. I know they commended their habits. But I wouldn't say that the Ku Klux Klan had any really big effect on Elk River. Just something to look at, something exciting. Elk River was pretty much of a fun town. Everybody worked hard. Saturday night they were looking for dances or something like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever cover this Helmer deal where they had the roller skating rink up there?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I've heard about that place. Old man Lawrence told me one time that people wouldn't pay their bills in the grocery store, so he decided to build a roller rink and spend their money for enjoyment. So he built that roller rink in the bygosh. They just come from all over for a long time. He had a swimming pool outside. And he had a swimming pool. And he even had a ball grounds figured out too. That's something we did when we were recording. We spent a lot of time every Saturday night in the summer over there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Went to Helmer, did you? What were those dances like? Did you really pack them in?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Oh, no, they had a good crowd.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: They moved that roller rink in Dance Hall to Troy after, and it burned down. And it was quite an active place for the university students from Moscow after that. Maybe somebody could tell you about it. Down around Moscow what happened later, but it finally got fire like most places. There's still a family of warts as in... I spoke to Floyd, one of the sons, about that. About that place in Helmer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did Malcolm Anderson kill himself before he left? What was that after?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I don't think Malcolm Anderson killed himself. He was still there. There's always been that controversy. Well, he was putting off some blasts on stumps or something on the highway or some blasting. They always cast aspersions about that, but I'll more or less defend them because I've been around mining camps. Some fuses burn off a fast and it doesn't give a fella a chance. He was a killer. I think you're probably thinking about his brother, Emil. Emilsona, yeah. I'm thinking of him. I don't know about Melker. I was out of there. Melker is the one. Milker died in his own property. That's interesting about Emil because I had heard that Emil had killed himself in that. Well, I heard that too. But I'm going to defend a man when he's putting off a blast like that because I know of a case up in Canada where a man lit a fuse. And even the fuses that they had left, they lit them at the inquest and they just went like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, why?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I don't know. But that can happen. And, you know, if a fuse goes that fast, it literally paralyzes the man to see something burn so fast like that. And I would defend the man that I don't think anybody is going to go through a blast.

speaker: Marie J. Clark
words: That Emil wasn't married, or did he marry that girl?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: He didn't marry that girl. But not that. But you're right. There's a question over that. Oh, I had heard that he killed himself. I mean, I had heard that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is there a divided opinion about Melker?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well, the trouble was I think that nobody could prove for sure whether he killed him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, he died by a gun blast, didn't he?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Right, and then Butchered wasn't there. Right in their own property. Yeah, practically in back of their own property. But that was the strange, he had a very strange wife. That's what I heard. Maggie Mae Jellyberg Anderson. Maggie Mae Christina Jellyberg Anderson was her name. I knew her brother when he was alive. He's... Yeah. That whole thing, if one could ever get the real gist of the whole story, it would be worthy of a book. And another thing, the Torgerson family. It says that could be a dine... Torgerson's had a dynasty. Yeah, a park. A park and quill, perhaps. And you know, Mr. and Mrs. Currier can give you a lot of information on that. Nearly every young girl that was raised in the park, when she was old enough, which was being 14 in those days, worked summers, or maybe all winter too, for the Torgessons. They could always work there so much, and they're bored. And they sure can tell a lot of stories. Lots of those girls.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you meet Bone?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: No, I haven't met George Torgerson. Oh, he can tell you some real stories about how he used to work for old man Torgerson. And he heard that horses, or tended horses, for old man Torgerson on Shasta Butte. And he got the handsome price of $6 a month in his keep. And he's got a lot of good stories. He was in Mount River before it was there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what is it about park that seems so interesting?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well, the dynasty that the Torgessons had, and how they brought people in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they bring a lot of those people into the area?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: They would come there anyway, and they would work for the Torgessons. But eventually they'd starve out. Why, even this viola was talking about working for some sawmill that's coming in. That's right, she was. And I think about 25 % of the people who lived in Elk River, more or less, have been involved in park at one time or another. A lot of those girls that came there to work in the camps, as flunkies, or even for waitresses, in Elk River, married within a year. Well, park's pretty isolated. I think there's about six people that live up there right now. But you've got this old man bone up here. You can tell a lot of the beginning stories. You've got old George, he can give you the Torgesson version of it. The interesting thing to me is, all these people went to work for the Torgessons, but when those young men were able to go and get away from home, they left. And even many, the youngest sister went to college here and taught a long time. She was married to Dr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Guy, the chiropractor, wasn't he?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: The chiropractor he was here. He's still alive.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why was she such a strange person?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, she used to be George Speck's girlfriend at one time too, didn't she?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Years back before they went into Elk River, I think, while they lived in park. George Smalls told me that she broke Eddie Erickson's heart. Yes, she did. That's true too. And she married Milka Anderson. That's right. I didn't know anything about that. Yep, she did. And Eddie never married me. Well, George said that he thinks that drove Eddie to really drink. He's really and truly was better off not having her for a life.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what was it about her?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: I mean, she sounds like a tease for one thing. Well, I don't know. I would contribute it to Ignorance.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how did she act? I mean, what did she act?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well, not exactly silly, but I don't think she ever carried on a good conversation. And I don't know whether she was afraid or self -conscious or what. She had three children. She lived in a very good wonder. Never at all. I was never with her in anything socially, I don't think. I believe she's a little older than I. I don't remember. I knew she was. But never was I with her that I can remember. And I don't know who she associated with up there. She was absolutely the opposite of Axel's wife. He had a darling wife and intelligent and good asset to the community. Nice children. I taught two of her children.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She died, though, didn't she?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: She died very young and left those six children.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that his first wife or her second wife?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: First.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did she die for, Dina?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Heart conditions. She was a heart worker and she was a reader. She'd read until three or four o 'clock in the morning. And I think she just overtaxed her heart. I don't think she ever took anything in the way of drugs or anything like that. She was active in all social affairs. She was super -p. And her two brothers came there to go to school. I don't know. I think their mother and father separated. Her father was an art... well, hers too. He was an artist. He's supposed to have written the book that we used at the college where our art force was here. One guy became from Southern Idaho. And these two brothers were quite a bit younger than she. And one was Manly and one was George, and they didn't look like brothers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was their last name?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Haunts. H -O -N -T -Z, if I remember. I should remember. That would be correct. And George was the one that I went coasting with, and Lucille went with Manly every night through the good coasting of that one winter. And so then, in the spring, they went back to their mother. And I don't know. They never came back again. Mrs. Anderson started getting children. And that just didn't give her enough time. This is Betty. Soon after they've been married for very long. Well, they were married in, I imagine, a whole year.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: But she wanted children. She had them just as fast as she could. A year apart, six real fast. Everybody had a fit. There's no trouble to her. And all the kids, one boy, the youngest boy, passed away a few years ago.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Where did Mr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Adams live?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Where did Mr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Adams live in California?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Oh, Eureka. Eureka. My mother was there one time. She'd been in South California for a visit. Her sister said she wanted to come home on a 101. So when they stopped in Eureka, she decided to stay over. Well, in fact, a former superintendent of schools, a black woman, went there. She was walking along the street, and somebody behind her called her name. And it was Richard Anderson. She was so pretty, so he came and got her and took her to his home when they had a real nice evening.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: And that's how many girls met their husbands by flunking in the camp. Oh, go up to the camp and flunking, and very soon they're marrying some guy. And coming to Elk River, started raising a family. But you know, they didn't do so bad. I didn't want to marry a mill hand or logging. But by the time those girls all did all right, we sure did. Good husbands, good homes, and good voters. Elk River sounds like it was really much more of a settled town than it was a lumberjack town in some ways. Well, don't sell it short. There was a lot of culture in Elk River. There were people that were good musicians. There were people that were good singers. And if they wanted to put on an entertainment in Elk River, it was good. I can remember the first one I saw there, and I just couldn't believe it was so much entertainment for the small community. They had a Christmas party or something. They went all out for it. And there were some beautiful singers at that time, Mrs. Burt Wagner and Mrs. Kars.

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Mary O 'Donnell. She was a musician. She played the piano a lot. They had singing and... Oh, they had a regular Christmas tree entertainment. Another group that gave a lot of entertainments in fact was the Ladies' Aid, the church. Oh, I never forget some of the plays they put on. I sure wish I could find the one that my mother was in when I was a little kid. You know, I made a big mistake. When her mother passed away, it wasn't your fault. She had a drawer about that big like that. Everything in there was all the programs that the Jockette kids were in or she was in or something else like that. They were all in this thing and I just... Any pro, all kinds of programs. Well, you asked me, and the way I looked at it, I said, Oh, bless, I did so much of that stuff myself that I don't even look at. Well, here were calling cards as they used to do this stuff. This is called calling cards.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You women use calling cards in their first quarter?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Yes, and at once, oh, anywhere all over the world, the United States then I think. And it was your duty. You were really obligated to go and call on practically everybody, especially your friends, once, and you maybe only stayed a half hour and you left your card.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How come you had to wait? Why use a card?

speaker: J. Les or Marie J. Clark
words: Well, to show that you had been there and that they were... No, no, it was just the formality of it. And everybody had a little dish that held these calling cards on her square living room table. You've seen these square ones that have the legs that go down like this and then a bottom and an under shelf, you know. They're all going to be antiques.
